(**NYC**),[[b]](#cite_note-14) is the [most populous city in the United States](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population"). It is located at the southern tip of [New York State](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") on [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), one of the world's largest natural harbors. The city comprises [five boroughs](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City"), each coextensive with [its respective county](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_New_York "List of counties in New York"). It is the geographical and demographic center of both the [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis") and the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"), the largest metropolitan area in the United States by both population and urban area. New York is a [global center](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") of [finance](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center")[[13]](#cite_note-NYCFinancialCapitalWorldA-15)[[14]](#cite_note-NYCFinancialCapitalWorldGFCI-16) and [commerce](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City"), [culture

**New York**, often called **New York City** (**NYC**),[[b]](#cite_note-14) is the [most populous city in the United States](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population"). It is located at the southern tip of [New York State](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") on [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), one of the world's largest natural harbors. The city comprises [five boroughs](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City"), each coextensive with [its respective county](/wiki/List_of_counties_in_New_York "List of counties in New York"). It is the geographical and demographic center of both the [Northeast megalopolis](/wiki/Northeast_megalopolis "Northeast megalopolis") and the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"), the largest metropolitan area in the United States by both population and urban area. New York is a [global center](/wiki/Global_city "Global city") of [finance](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center")[[13]](#cite_note-NYCFinancialCapitalWorldA-15)[[14]](#cite_note-NYCFinancialCapitalWorldGFCI-16) and [commerce](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City"), [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_New_York_City "Culture of New York City"), [technology](/wiki/High_technology "High technology"),[[15]](#cite_note-NewYorkCityDestinationNumberOneTechHub-17) [entertainment](/wiki/The_Entertainment_Capital_of_the_World "The Entertainment Capital of the World") and [media](/wiki/Media_in_New_York_City "Media in New York City"), [academics](/wiki/Academy "Academy") and [scientific output](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_scientific_output "List of cities by scientific output"),[[16]](#cite_note-NYCScientificCapital-18) the arts and fashion, and, as home to the [headquarters of the United Nations](/wiki/Headquarters_of_the_United_Nations "Headquarters of the United Nations"), international diplomacy.[[c]](#cite_note-24)

With a [Census](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau")-estimated population in July 2025 of 8,584,629, distributed over 300.46 square miles (778.2 km2),[[6]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-7) the city is the [most densely populated](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population_density "List of United States cities by population density") major city in the United States.[[22]](#cite_note-NYCQuickFacts-25)[[8]](#cite_note-nyc.gov-9) New York City has more than double the population of [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"), the country's second-most populous city.[[23]](#cite_note-CensusNYC-26) Over 20.1 million people live in New York City's [metropolitan statistical area](/wiki/Metropolitan_statistical_area "Metropolitan statistical area")[[24]](#cite_note-27) and 23.5 million in its [combined statistical area](/wiki/Combined_statistical_area "Combined statistical area") as of 2020, both the largest in the U.S. New York City is one of the world's most populous [megacities](/wiki/Megacity "Megacity").[[25]](#cite_note-28) The city and its metropolitan area serve as the premier gateway for legal [immigration to the United States](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States"). An estimated 800 languages are spoken in New York City,[[26]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sMostCulturallyLinguisticallyDiverseCityTop-29)[[27]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sMostLinguisticallyDiverseCity3Top-30)[[28]](#cite_note-QueensMostLinguisticallyDiverseTop-31) making it the most linguistically diverse city in the world.[[29]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sMostLinguisticallyDiverseCity1-32)[[30]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sMostLinguisticallyDiverseCity2-33) The New York City metropolitan region is home to the [largest foreign-born population of any metropolitan region in the world](/wiki/Foreign_born#Metropolitan_and_urban_regions_with_the_largest_foreign-born_populations "Foreign born"), approximately 5.9 million as of 2023.

New York City traces [its origins](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City "History of New York City") to [Fort Amsterdam](/wiki/Fort_Amsterdam "Fort Amsterdam") and a trading post founded on Manhattan Island by [Dutch colonists](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas") around 1624. The settlement was named [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") in 1626 and was [chartered](/wiki/Charter "Charter") as a city in 1653. The city came under [English control](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas") in 1664 and was temporarily renamed New York after King [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England") granted the lands to his brother, the [Duke of York](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England"),[[31]](#cite_note-npsnetherland-34) before this name was permanently adopted in 1674. Following independence from Great Britain, the city was the [national capital of the United States](/wiki/List_of_capitals_in_the_United_States "List of capitals in the United States") from 1785 until 1790.[[32]](#cite_note-senate-35) The modern city was formed by the [1898 consolidation](/wiki/Consolidation_of_New_York_City "Consolidation of New York City") of its five [boroughs](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City"): [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens"), [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island").

Anchored by [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") in the [Financial District, Manhattan](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan"), New York City has been called both the world's premier financial and [fintech](/wiki/Fintech "Fintech") center[[33]](#cite_note-NYCFinancialAndFintechCapitalWorld-36)[[34]](#cite_note-reuters-2022-03-24-37) and the most economically powerful city in the world.[[35]](#cite_note-NewYorkMostPowerfulGlobalCity-38) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit), the New York metropolitan area is the [largest metropolitan economy in the world](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP"), with a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/List_of_U.S._metropolitan_areas_by_GDP "List of U.S. metropolitan areas by GDP") of over US$2.16 trillion.[[12]](#cite_note-fred.stlouisfed.org-13) The New York metropolitan area's economy is [larger than all but nine countries](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)"). Despite having [a 24/7 rapid transit system](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"), New York also leads the world in urban automobile [traffic congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion").[[36]](#cite_note-NYCCongestion-39) The city is home to the world's two [largest stock exchanges](/wiki/List_of_stock_exchanges "List of stock exchanges") by [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of their listed companies: the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") and [Nasdaq](/wiki/Nasdaq "Nasdaq"). New York City is an established haven for global investors.[[37]](#cite_note-NYCSafeHavenGlobalInvestors-40) As of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit), New York City is the most expensive city in the world for [expatriates](/wiki/Expatriate "Expatriate")[[38]](#cite_note-MostExpensiveCityExpats-41) and has by a wide margin the highest residential rents of any American city.[[39]](#cite_note-NYCMostExpensiveRents-42) [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") is the most expensive shopping street in the world.[[40]](#cite_note-FifthAvenueMostExpensiveStreetOnEarth-43) New York City is home to the [highest number of billionaires](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_number_of_billionaires "List of cities by number of billionaires"),[[41]](#cite_note-NYCHighestNumberBillionaires-44) individuals of [ultra-high net worth](/wiki/High_net_worth_individual "High net worth individual") (greater than US$30 million),[[42]](#cite_note-NYCUltraHighNetWorth-45) and [millionaires](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_number_of_millionaires "List of cities by number of millionaires") of any city in the world by a significant margin.[[43]](#cite_note-NewYorkArtMarketGlobalHeadquarters-46)

## Etymology

See also: [Nicknames of New York City](/wiki/Nicknames_of_New_York_City "Nicknames of New York City")

In 1664, New York was named in honor of the [Duke of York](/wiki/Duke_of_York "Duke of York") (later King [James II of England](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England")).[[44]](#cite_note-47) James's elder brother, King [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II_of_England "Charles II of England"), appointed him [proprietor](/wiki/Proprietary_colony "Proprietary colony") of the former territory of [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland"), including the city of [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam"), when England [seized](/wiki/Conquest_of_New_Netherland "Conquest of New Netherland") it from [Dutch control](/wiki/Dutch_colonization_of_the_Americas "Dutch colonization of the Americas").[[45]](#cite_note-Archdeacon2013a-48) New Netherland was renamed the [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York") (now [New York State](/wiki/New_York_State "New York State")).[[46]](#cite_note-49)

## History

Main articles: [History of New York City](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City "History of New York City") and [Timeline of New York City](/wiki/Timeline_of_New_York_City "Timeline of New York City")

Further information: [History of Manhattan](/wiki/History_of_Manhattan "History of Manhattan"), [Timeline of Brooklyn](/wiki/Timeline_of_Brooklyn "Timeline of Brooklyn"), [Timeline of Queens](/wiki/Timeline_of_Queens "Timeline of Queens"), [Timeline of the Bronx](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_Bronx "Timeline of the Bronx"), and [Timeline of Staten Island](/wiki/Timeline_of_Staten_Island "Timeline of Staten Island")

### Early history

Main article: [History of New York City (prehistory–1664)](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(prehistory%E2%80%931664) "History of New York City (prehistory–1664)")

In the [pre-Columbian era](/wiki/Pre-Columbian_era "Pre-Columbian era"), the area of present-day New York City was inhabited by [Algonquians](/wiki/Algonquian_peoples "Algonquian peoples"), including the [Lenape](/wiki/Lenape "Lenape"). Their homeland, known as [Lenapehoking](/wiki/Lenapehoking "Lenapehoking"), included the present-day areas of [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island"), [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), [the Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx"), the western portion of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island") (including [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") and [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens")), and the [Lower Hudson Valley](/wiki/Lower_Hudson_Valley "Lower Hudson Valley").[[47]](#cite_note-50)

The first documented visit to [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") by a European was in 1524 by explorer [Giovanni da Verrazzano](/wiki/Giovanni_da_Verrazzano "Giovanni da Verrazzano").[[48]](#cite_note-Debo2013-51) He claimed the area for [France](/wiki/Kingdom_of_France "Kingdom of France") and named it *Nouvelle Angoulême* (New [Angoulême](/wiki/Angoul%C3%AAme "Angoulême")).[[49]](#cite_note-rodgers-52) A Spanish expedition, led by the Portuguese captain [Estêvão Gomes](/wiki/Est%C3%AAv%C3%A3o_Gomes "Estêvão Gomes") sailing for [Emperor Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor"), arrived in New York Harbor in January 1525 and charted the mouth of the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), which he named *Río de San Antonio* ('Saint Anthony's River').[[50]](#cite_note-53)

In 1609, the English explorer [Henry Hudson](/wiki/Henry_Hudson "Henry Hudson") rediscovered New York Harbor while searching for the [Northwest Passage](/wiki/Northwest_Passage "Northwest Passage") to the [Orient](/wiki/Orient "Orient") for the [Dutch East India Company](/wiki/Dutch_East_India_Company "Dutch East India Company").[[51]](#cite_note-Lankevich2002-54) He sailed up what the Dutch called [North River](/wiki/North_River_(Hudson_River) "North River (Hudson River)") (now the Hudson River), named first by Hudson as the *Mauritius* after [Maurice, Prince of Orange](/wiki/Maurice,_Prince_of_Orange "Maurice, Prince of Orange").[[52]](#cite_note-hudsonnni-55)

Hudson claimed the region for the Dutch East India Company. In 1614, the area between [Cape Cod](/wiki/Cape_Cod "Cape Cod") and [Delaware Bay](/wiki/Delaware_Bay "Delaware Bay") was claimed by the Netherlands and named *Nieuw-Nederland* ("[New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland")"). The first non–Native American inhabitant of what became New York City was [Juan Rodriguez](/wiki/Juan_(Jan)_Rodriguez "Juan (Jan) Rodriguez"), a merchant from [Santo Domingo](/wiki/Captaincy_General_of_Santo_Domingo "Captaincy General of Santo Domingo") who arrived in Manhattan during the winter of 1613–14, trapping for [pelts](/wiki/Fur "Fur") and trading with the local population as a representative of the Dutch.[[53]](#cite_note-56)[[54]](#cite_note-57)

### Dutch rule

Main articles: [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam"), [Fort Amsterdam](/wiki/Fort_Amsterdam "Fort Amsterdam"), and [New Netherland](/wiki/New_Netherland "New Netherland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Stad_Amsterdam_in_Nieuw_Nederland_%28City_Amsterdam_in_New_Netherland%29_Castello_Plan_1660.jpg/250px-Stad_Amsterdam_in_Nieuw_Nederland_%28City_Amsterdam_in_New_Netherland%29_Castello_Plan_1660.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stad_Amsterdam_in_Nieuw_Nederland_(City_Amsterdam_in_New_Netherland)_Castello_Plan_1660.jpg)

The [Castello Plan](/wiki/Castello_Plan "Castello Plan"), a 1660 map of [New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam") in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/GezichtOpNieuwAmsterdam.jpg/250px-GezichtOpNieuwAmsterdam.jpg)](/wiki/File:GezichtOpNieuwAmsterdam.jpg)

[New Amsterdam](/wiki/New_Amsterdam "New Amsterdam"), centered in what eventually became Lower Manhattan, in 1664, the year [England](/wiki/British_colonization_of_the_Americas "British colonization of the Americas") took control and renamed it New York

A permanent European presence near [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor") was established in 1624, making New York the [12th-oldest continuously occupied](/wiki/List_of_North_American_settlements_by_year_of_foundation "List of North American settlements by year of foundation") European-established settlement in the [continental United States](/wiki/Continental_United_States "Continental United States"), with the founding of a Dutch [fur trading](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") settlement on [Governors Island](/wiki/Governors_Island "Governors Island"). In 1625, construction was started on a [citadel](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel") and [Fort Amsterdam](/wiki/Fort_Amsterdam "Fort Amsterdam"), later called *Nieuw Amsterdam* (New Amsterdam), on present-day Manhattan Island.[[55]](#cite_note-58)[[56]](#cite_note-Tolerance-59)

The colony of New Amsterdam extended from the southern tip of Manhattan to modern-day [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"), where a 12-foot (3.7 m) wooden [stockade](/wiki/Stockade "Stockade") was built in 1653 to protect against Native American and English raids.[[57]](#cite_note-60) In 1626 [Peter Minuit](/wiki/Peter_Minuit "Peter Minuit"), the director of New Netherland, as charged by the [Dutch West India Company](/wiki/Dutch_West_India_Company "Dutch West India Company"), purchased the island of Manhattan from the *Canarsie*, a small Lenape band,[[58]](#cite_note-61) for "the value of 60 [guilders](/wiki/Dutch_guilder "Dutch guilder")"[[59]](#cite_note-62) (about $900 in 2018).[[60]](#cite_note-63) A frequently told but disproved legend claims that Manhattan was purchased for $24 worth of glass beads.[[61]](#cite_note-64)[[62]](#cite_note-65)

Following the purchase, New Amsterdam grew slowly.[[31]](#cite_note-npsnetherland-34) To attract settlers, the Dutch instituted the [patroon system](/wiki/Patroon "Patroon") in 1628, whereby wealthy Dutchmen (*patroons*, or patrons) who brought 50 colonists to New Netherland would be awarded land, local political autonomy, and rights to participate in the lucrative fur trade. This program had little success.[[63]](#cite_note-locnetherland-66)

Since 1621, the Dutch West India Company had operated as a monopoly in New Netherland, on authority granted by the [Dutch States General](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands"). In 1639–1640, to bolster economic growth, the Dutch West India Company relinquished its monopoly over the fur trade, leading to growth in the production and trade of food, timber, tobacco, and slaves (particularly with the [Dutch West Indies](/wiki/Netherlands_Antilles "Netherlands Antilles")).[[31]](#cite_note-npsnetherland-34)[[64]](#cite_note-nahcnetherland-67)

In 1647, [Peter Stuyvesant](/wiki/Peter_Stuyvesant "Peter Stuyvesant") began his tenure as the last [director-general](/wiki/Directors_of_New_Netherland "Directors of New Netherland") of New Netherland. During his tenure, the population of New Netherland grew from 2,000 to 8,000.[[65]](#cite_note-68)[[66]](#cite_note-69) Stuyvesant has been credited with improving law and order; however, he earned a reputation as a [despotic](/wiki/Despotism "Despotism") leader. He instituted regulations on liquor sales, attempted to assert control over the [Dutch Reformed Church](/wiki/Dutch_Reformed_Church "Dutch Reformed Church"), and blocked other religious groups from establishing houses of worship.[[67]](#cite_note-nyhsstuyvesant-70)

### English rule

Main articles: [Province of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York") and [History of New York City (1665–1783)](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1665%E2%80%931783) "History of New York City (1665–1783)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/The_fall_of_New_Amsterdam_cph.3g12217.jpg/250px-The_fall_of_New_Amsterdam_cph.3g12217.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_fall_of_New_Amsterdam_cph.3g12217.jpg)

*[The Fall of New Amsterdam](/wiki/The_Fall_of_New_Amsterdam "The Fall of New Amsterdam")*, painting by [Jean Leon Gerome Ferris](/wiki/Jean_Leon_Gerome_Ferris "Jean Leon Gerome Ferris"), depicting the [Conquest of New Netherland](/wiki/Conquest_of_New_Netherland "Conquest of New Netherland")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/A_view_of_Fort_George_with_the_city_of_New_York%2C_from_the_SW.jpg/250px-A_view_of_Fort_George_with_the_city_of_New_York%2C_from_the_SW.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_view_of_Fort_George_with_the_city_of_New_York,_from_the_SW.jpg)

[Fort George](/wiki/Fort_Amsterdam "Fort Amsterdam") and New York with British warships, c. 1731

In 1664, unable to summon any significant resistance, Stuyvesant surrendered New Amsterdam to English troops, led by Colonel [Richard Nicolls](/wiki/Richard_Nicolls "Richard Nicolls"), without bloodshed.[[67]](#cite_note-nyhsstuyvesant-70)[[68]](#cite_note-nnistuyvesant-71) The terms of the surrender permitted Dutch residents to remain in the colony and allowed for religious freedom.[[69]](#cite_note-lehrmanstuyvesant-72)

In 1667, during negotiations leading to the [Treaty of Breda](/wiki/Treaty_of_Breda_(1667) "Treaty of Breda (1667)") after the [Second Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Dutch_War "Second Anglo-Dutch War"), the victorious Dutch decided to keep the nascent plantation colony of what is now [Suriname](/wiki/Suriname "Suriname"), which they had gained from the English,[[70]](#cite_note-73) and in return the English kept New Amsterdam. The settlement was promptly renamed "New York" after the [Duke of York](/wiki/James_II_of_England "James II of England") (the future King James II and VII).[[2]](#cite_note-homberger400-2) The duke gave part of the colony to proprietors [George Carteret](/wiki/George_Carteret "George Carteret") and [John Berkeley](/wiki/John_Berkeley,_1st_Baron_Berkeley_of_Stratton "John Berkeley, 1st Baron Berkeley of Stratton").[[71]](#cite_note-74)

On August 24, 1673, during the [Third Anglo-Dutch War](/wiki/Third_Anglo-Dutch_War "Third Anglo-Dutch War"), [Anthony Colve](/wiki/Anthony_Colve "Anthony Colve") of the Dutch navy [seized New York](/wiki/Dutch_Raid_on_North_America "Dutch Raid on North America") at the behest of [Cornelis Evertsen the Youngest](/wiki/Cornelis_Evertsen_the_Youngest "Cornelis Evertsen the Youngest") and rechristened it "New Orange" after [William III](/wiki/William_III_of_England "William III of England"), the [Prince of Orange](/wiki/Prince_of_Orange "Prince of Orange").[[72]](#cite_note-Roper2017-75) The Dutch soon returned the island to England under the [Treaty of Westminster](/wiki/Treaty_of_Westminster_(1674) "Treaty of Westminster (1674)") of November 1674.[[73]](#cite_note-76)[[74]](#cite_note-77)

Several intertribal wars among the Native Americans and epidemics brought on by contact with the Europeans caused sizeable population losses for the Lenape between 1660 and 1670.[[75]](#cite_note-78) By 1700, the Lenape population had diminished to 200.[[76]](#cite_note-79) New York experienced several [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever") epidemics in the 18th century, losing ten percent of its population in 1702 alone.[[77]](#cite_note-80)[[78]](#cite_note-81)

In the early 18th century, New York grew in importance as a trading port as a part of the [colony of New York](/wiki/Province_of_New_York "Province of New York").[[79]](#cite_note-Foote2004-82) It became a center of [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_colonial_United_States "Slavery in the colonial United States"), with 42% of households enslaving Africans by 1730.[[80]](#cite_note-83) Most were [domestic slaves](/wiki/House_slave "House slave"); others were hired out as labor. Slavery became integrally tied to New York's economy through the labor of slaves throughout the port, and the banking and shipping industries trading with the [American South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"). During construction in [Foley Square](/wiki/Foley_Square "Foley Square") in the 1990s, the [African Burying Ground](/wiki/African_Burying_Ground "African Burying Ground") was discovered; the cemetery included 10,000 to 20,000 graves of colonial-era Africans, some enslaved and some free.[[81]](#cite_note-AsanteMazama2005-84)

The 1735 trial and acquittal in Manhattan of [John Peter Zenger](/wiki/John_Peter_Zenger "John Peter Zenger"), who had been accused of [seditious libel](/wiki/Seditious_libel "Seditious libel") after criticizing [colonial governor](/wiki/List_of_colonial_governors_of_New_York "List of colonial governors of New York") [William Cosby](/wiki/William_Cosby "William Cosby"), helped to establish [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press") in North America.[[82]](#cite_note-zenger-85) In 1754, [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") was founded.[[83]](#cite_note-86)

### American Revolution

Further information: [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/BattleofLongisland.jpg/250px-BattleofLongisland.jpg)](/wiki/File:BattleofLongisland.jpg)

The [Battle of Long Island](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Island "Battle of Long Island"), one of the largest battles of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), which took place in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") on August 27, 1776

The [Stamp Act Congress](/wiki/Stamp_Act_Congress "Stamp Act Congress") met in New York in October 1765, as the [Sons of Liberty](/wiki/Sons_of_Liberty "Sons of Liberty") organization emerged in the city and skirmished over the next ten years with British troops stationed there.[[84]](#cite_note-BoyerClark2009-87) The [Battle of Long Island](/wiki/Battle_of_Long_Island "Battle of Long Island"), the largest battle of the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), was fought in August 1776 within modern-day Brooklyn.[[85]](#cite_note-Reno2008-88) A British rout of the Continental Army at the [Battle of Fort Washington](/wiki/Battle_of_Fort_Washington "Battle of Fort Washington") in November 1776 eliminated the last American stronghold in Manhattan, causing [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington") and his forces to retreat across the Hudson River to [New Jersey](/wiki/New_Jersey "New Jersey"), pursued by British forces.[[86]](#cite_note-89)[[87]](#cite_note-90)

After the battle, in which the Americans were defeated, the British made New York their military and political base of operations in North America.[[88]](#cite_note-91) The city was a haven for [Loyalist](/wiki/Loyalist_(American_Revolution) "Loyalist (American Revolution)") refugees and escaped slaves who joined the British lines for freedom promised by the [Crown](/wiki/British_Crown "British Crown"), with as many as 10,000 escaped slaves crowded into the city during the British occupation, the largest such community on the continent.[[89]](#cite_note-92)[[90]](#cite_note-93) When the British forces [evacuated](/wiki/Evacuation_Day_(New_York) "Evacuation Day (New York)") New York at the close of the war in 1783, they transported thousands of [freedmen](/wiki/Freedmen "Freedmen") for resettlement in [Nova Scotia](/wiki/Nova_Scotia "Nova Scotia"), England, and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean").[[91]](#cite_note-Hinks2007-94)

The attempt at a peaceful solution to the war took place at the [Conference House](/wiki/Conference_House "Conference House") on Staten Island between American delegates, including [Benjamin Franklin](/wiki/Benjamin_Franklin "Benjamin Franklin"), and British general [Lord Howe](/wiki/Richard_Howe,_1st_Earl_Howe "Richard Howe, 1st Earl Howe") on September 11, 1776.[[92]](#cite_note-95) Shortly after the British occupation began, the [Great Fire of New York](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_New_York_(1776) "Great Fire of New York (1776)") destroyed nearly 500 buildings, about a quarter of the structures in the city, including [Trinity Church](/wiki/Trinity_Church_(New_York_City) "Trinity Church (New York City)").[[93]](#cite_note-96)[[94]](#cite_note-97)

### Post-revolutionary period and early 19th century

Main article: [History of New York City (1784–1854)](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1784%E2%80%931854) "History of New York City (1784–1854)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e6/Ramon_Elorriaga_-_Inauguration_of_George_Washington.jpg/250px-Ramon_Elorriaga_-_Inauguration_of_George_Washington.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ramon_Elorriaga_-_Inauguration_of_George_Washington.jpg)

A portrait of the [first inauguration of George Washington](/wiki/First_inauguration_of_George_Washington "First inauguration of George Washington") in 1789

In January 1785, the assembly of the [Congress of the Confederation](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Confederation "Congress of the Confederation") made New York City the national capital.[[95]](#cite_note-98) New York was the last capital of the United States under the [Articles of Confederation](/wiki/Articles_of_Confederation "Articles of Confederation") and the first under the [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_United_States "Constitution of the United States").[[96]](#cite_note-Post-Revolutionary_War-99) As the capital, New York City hosted the inauguration of the first President, [George Washington](/wiki/George_Washington "George Washington"), and the first [Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress"), at [Federal Hall](/wiki/Federal_Hall "Federal Hall") on [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"). Congress drafted the [Bill of Rights](/wiki/United_States_Bill_of_Rights "United States Bill of Rights") there.[[96]](#cite_note-Post-Revolutionary_War-99) The [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") held its first organizational sessions in New York in 1790.

In 1790, for the first time, New York City surpassed [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") as the nation's largest city. At the end of 1790, the national capital was [moved to Philadelphia](/wiki/Residence_Act "Residence Act"), where it remained while the new capital in Washington, D.C. was being constructed.[[97]](#cite_note-residence_act-100)[[32]](#cite_note-senate-35)

During the 19th century New York City's population grew from 60,000 to 3.43 million.[[98]](#cite_note-101) Under New York State's [gradual emancipation](/wiki/Gradual_emancipation_(United_States) "Gradual emancipation (United States)") act of 1799, children of slave mothers were to be eventually liberated but to be held in [indentured servitude](/wiki/Indentured_servitude "Indentured servitude") until their mid-to-late twenties.[[99]](#cite_note-102)[[100]](#cite_note-103) A significant free Black population gradually developed in Manhattan, made up of former slaves who had been freed by their masters after the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"), as well as escaped slaves. The [New York Manumission Society](/wiki/New_York_Manumission_Society "New York Manumission Society") worked for abolition and established the [African Free School](/wiki/African_Free_School "African Free School") to educate Black children.[[101]](#cite_note-Divided-104) It was not until 1827 that [slavery was completely abolished in the state](/wiki/History_of_slavery_in_New_York_(state) "History of slavery in New York (state)").[[102]](#cite_note-105) Free Blacks struggled with discrimination, and interracial abolitionist activism continued. New York City's population jumped from 123,706 in 1820 (10,886 of whom were Black and of whom 518 were enslaved) to 312,710 by 1840 (16,358 of whom were Black).[[103]](#cite_note-Census1790to1990-106)

[![A painting of a snowy city street with horse-drawn sleds and a 19th-century fire truck under blue sky](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Hippolyte_Sebron_-_Rue_De_New-York_En_1840.jpg/250px-Hippolyte_Sebron_-_Rue_De_New-York_En_1840.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hippolyte_Sebron_-_Rue_De_New-York_En_1840.jpg)

[Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)"), which follows the Native American [Wecquaesgeek](/wiki/Wecquaesgeek "Wecquaesgeek") Trail through Manhattan, 1840[[104]](#cite_note-107)

Also in the 19th century, the city was transformed by both commercial and residential development relating to its status as a national and international trading center, as well as by European immigration, respectively.[[105]](#cite_note-Rosenwaike30-108): [55](https://archive.org/details/populationhistor00irar/page/55) The city adopted the [Commissioners' Plan of 1811](/wiki/Commissioners%27_Plan_of_1811 "Commissioners' Plan of 1811"), which expanded the city [street grid](/wiki/Grid_plan#Early_United_States_(17th–19th_centuries) "Grid plan") to encompass almost all of Manhattan. The 1825 completion of the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") through [central New York](/wiki/Central_New_York "Central New York") connected the [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") port to the agricultural markets and commodities of the North American interior via the Hudson River and the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes").[[106]](#cite_note-109) Local politics became dominated by [Tammany Hall](/wiki/Tammany_Hall "Tammany Hall"), a [political machine](/wiki/Political_machine "Political machine") supported by [Irish](/wiki/Irish_diaspora "Irish diaspora") and [German immigrants](/wiki/German_diaspora "German diaspora").[[107]](#cite_note-110) In 1831, [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") was founded.[[108]](#cite_note-111)

Several prominent American literary figures lived in New York during the 1830s and 1840s, including [William Cullen Bryant](/wiki/William_Cullen_Bryant "William Cullen Bryant"), [Washington Irving](/wiki/Washington_Irving "Washington Irving"), [Herman Melville](/wiki/Herman_Melville "Herman Melville"), [Rufus Wilmot Griswold](/wiki/Rufus_Wilmot_Griswold "Rufus Wilmot Griswold"), [John Keese](/wiki/John_Keese "John Keese"), [Nathaniel Parker Willis](/wiki/Nathaniel_Parker_Willis "Nathaniel Parker Willis"), and [Edgar Allan Poe](/wiki/Edgar_Allan_Poe "Edgar Allan Poe"). Members of the business elite lobbied for the establishment of [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"), which in 1857 became the first [landscaped park](/wiki/Landscape_design "Landscape design") in an American city.[[109]](#cite_note-112)

The [Great Irish Famine](/wiki/Great_Famine_(Ireland) "Great Famine (Ireland)") brought a large influx of Irish immigrants, of whom more than 200,000 were living in New York by 1860, representing over a quarter of the city's population.[[110]](#cite_note-113) Extensive immigration from the German provinces meant that Germans comprised another 25% of New York's population by 1860.[[111]](#cite_note-Harris-114)[[112]](#cite_note-115)

### American Civil War

Main articles: [New York City in the American Civil War](/wiki/New_York_City_in_the_American_Civil_War "New York City in the American Civil War") and [History of New York City (1855–1897)](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1855%E2%80%931897) "History of New York City (1855–1897)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/The_Departure_of_the_7th_Regiment.jpg/250px-The_Departure_of_the_7th_Regiment.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Departure_of_the_7th_Regiment.jpg)

Departure of the [7th New York Militia Regiment](/wiki/7th_New_York_Militia_Regiment "7th New York Militia Regiment") for the defense of Washington, D.C., April 19, 1861

[Democratic Party](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") candidates were consistently elected to local office, increasing the city's ties to the South and its dominant party. In 1861, Mayor [Fernando Wood](/wiki/Fernando_Wood "Fernando Wood") called on the [aldermen](/wiki/Alderman "Alderman") to declare independence from Albany and the United States after the South seceded, but his proposal was not acted on.[[101]](#cite_note-Divided-104) Anger at new [military conscription](/wiki/Military_conscription "Military conscription") laws during the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") (1861–1865), which spared wealthier men who could afford to hire a substitute, led to the [Draft Riots of 1863](/wiki/New_York_City_draft_riots "New York City draft riots"), whose most visible participants were ethnic Irish working class.[[101]](#cite_note-Divided-104)

The draft riots deteriorated into attacks on New York's elite, followed by attacks on Black New Yorkers after fierce competition for a decade between Irish immigrants and Black people for work. Rioters burned the Colored Orphan Asylum to the ground.[[111]](#cite_note-Harris-114) At least 120 people were killed.[[113]](#cite_note-McPherson-116) Eleven Black men were lynched over five days, and the riots forced hundreds of Blacks to flee. The Black population in Manhattan fell below 10,000 by 1865. The White working class had established dominance.[[111]](#cite_note-Harris-114)[[113]](#cite_note-McPherson-116) It was one of the worst incidents of [civil unrest in American history](/wiki/List_of_incidents_of_civil_unrest_in_the_United_States "List of incidents of civil unrest in the United States").[[114]](#cite_note-117)

### Late 19th and early 20th century

Main articles: [History of New York City (1898–1945)](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1898%E2%80%931945) "History of New York City (1898–1945)") and [History of New York City (1946–1977)](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1946%E2%80%931977) "History of New York City (1946–1977)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg/250px-Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mulberry_Street_NYC_c1900_LOC_3g04637u_edit.jpg)

Manhattan's [Little Italy](/wiki/Little_Italy,_Manhattan "Little Italy, Manhattan") c. 1900

In 1886, the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty"), a gift from France, was dedicated in New York Harbor. The statue welcomed 14 million immigrants as they arrived via [Ellis Island](/wiki/Ellis_Island "Ellis Island") by ship in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, and is a symbol of the United States and American ideals of liberty and peace.[[115]](#cite_note-Statue_of_Liberty_UNESCO-118)[[116]](#cite_note-119)

In 1898, the City of New York was formed with the [consolidation](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York") of Brooklyn (until then a separate city), the County of New York (which then included parts of the Bronx), the County of Richmond, and the western portion of the County of Queens.[[117]](#cite_note-120) The opening of the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") in 1904, first built as separate private systems, helped bind the new city together.[[118]](#cite_note-Cudahy2004-121) Throughout the first half of the 20th century, the city became a world center for industry, commerce, and communication.[[119]](#cite_note-Blake2009-122)

In 1904, the [steamship](/wiki/Steamship "Steamship") *[General Slocum](/wiki/PS_General_Slocum "PS General Slocum")* caught fire in the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River"), killing 1,021 people.[[120]](#cite_note-Sheard1998-123) In 1911, the [Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire](/wiki/Triangle_Shirtwaist_Factory_fire "Triangle Shirtwaist Factory fire"), the city's worst industrial disaster, killed 146 garment workers and spurred the growth of the [International Ladies' Garment Workers' Union](/wiki/International_Ladies%27_Garment_Workers%27_Union "International Ladies' Garment Workers' Union") and major improvements in building safety standards.[[121]](#cite_note-cornell1-124)

[![A man working on a steel girder high above a city skyline.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Old_timer_structural_worker2.jpg/250px-Old_timer_structural_worker2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Old_timer_structural_worker2.jpg)

A [construction worker](/wiki/Construction_worker "Construction worker") atop the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building") during its construction in 1930. The [Chrysler Building](/wiki/Chrysler_Building "Chrysler Building") is visible to the right.

New York's non-White population was 36,620 in 1890.[[105]](#cite_note-Rosenwaike30-108): Table 30 New York City was a prime destination in the early 20th century for Blacks during the [Great Migration](/wiki/Great_Migration_(African_American) "Great Migration (African American)") from the American South, and by 1916, New York City had the largest urban [African diaspora](/wiki/African_diaspora "African diaspora") in North America.[[122]](#cite_note-GatesHigginbotham2009-125) The [Harlem Renaissance](/wiki/Harlem_Renaissance "Harlem Renaissance") of literary and [cultural life](/wiki/Culture_of_New_York_City "Culture of New York City") flourished during the era of [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States").[[123]](#cite_note-Roche2015-126) The larger economic boom generated the construction of skyscrapers competing in height.[[124]](#cite_note-127)

New York City became the most populous [urbanized area](/wiki/Urban_area#United_States "Urban area") in the world in the early 1920s, overtaking London. The metropolitan area surpassed 10 million in the early 1930s, becoming the first [megacity](/wiki/Megacity "Megacity").[[125]](#cite_note-128) The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") saw the election of reformer [Fiorello La Guardia](/wiki/Fiorello_La_Guardia "Fiorello La Guardia") as mayor and the fall of Tammany Hall after eighty years of political dominance.[[126]](#cite_note-129)

Returning [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") veterans created a post-war [economic boom](/wiki/Business_cycle "Business cycle") and the development of large [housing tracts](/wiki/Housing_tract "Housing tract") in eastern Queens and [Nassau County](/wiki/Nassau_County,_New_York "Nassau County, New York"), with Wall Street leading America's place as the world's dominant economic power. The [United Nations headquarters](/wiki/United_Nations_headquarters "United Nations headquarters") was completed in 1952, solidifying New York's global [geopolitical](/wiki/Geopolitical "Geopolitical") influence, and the rise of [abstract expressionism](/wiki/Abstract_expressionism "Abstract expressionism") in the city precipitated New York's displacement of Paris as the center of the art world.[[127]](#cite_note-130)

### Late 20th and early 21st centuries

Main articles: [History of New York City (1978–present)](/wiki/History_of_New_York_City_(1978%E2%80%93present) "History of New York City (1978–present)") and [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks")

[![A two-story building with brick on the first floor, with two arched doorways, and gray stucco on the second floor, off of which hang numerous rainbow flags.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/Stonewall_Inn_5_pride_weekend_2016.jpg/250px-Stonewall_Inn_5_pride_weekend_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stonewall_Inn_5_pride_weekend_2016.jpg)

[Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn") in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), the site of the June 1969 [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots") and the cradle of the modern [LGBTQ+ rights](/wiki/Gay_rights "Gay rights") movement[[128]](#cite_note-GayGreenwichVillage1-131)[[129]](#cite_note-132)[[130]](#cite_note-133)

In 1969, the [Stonewall riots](/wiki/Stonewall_riots "Stonewall riots") were a series of violent protests by members of the [gay community](/wiki/LGBT_community "LGBT community") against a [police raid](/wiki/Police_raid "Police raid") that took place in the early morning of June 28, 1969, at the [Stonewall Inn](/wiki/Stonewall_Inn "Stonewall Inn") in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village").[[131]](#cite_note-Murphy2013-134) They are widely considered to be the single most important event leading to the [gay liberation](/wiki/Gay_liberation "Gay liberation") movement[[128]](#cite_note-GayGreenwichVillage1-131)[[132]](#cite_note-KentuckyStonewall-135)[[133]](#cite_note-PinkNewsStonewall-136)[[134]](#cite_note-EncycloStonewall-137) and the modern fight for [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_by_country_or_territory "LGBT rights by country or territory").[[135]](#cite_note-NPSStonewall-138)[[136]](#cite_note-ObamaStonewall-139) [Wayne R. Dynes](/wiki/Wayne_R._Dynes "Wayne R. Dynes"), author of the *[Encyclopedia of Homosexuality](/wiki/Encyclopedia_of_Homosexuality "Encyclopedia of Homosexuality")*, wrote that [drag queens](/wiki/Drag_queen "Drag queen") were the only "[transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") folks around" during the Stonewall riots. The transgender community in New York City played a significant role in fighting for LGBT equality.[[137]](#cite_note-TransEqualityNYC-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/77/Ford_to_City.PNG/250px-Ford_to_City.PNG)](/wiki/File:Ford_to_City.PNG)

October 1975 *[New York Daily News](/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")* front page on President Ford's refusal to help the city avert bankruptcy

In the 1970s, job losses due to [industrial restructuring](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization") caused New York City to suffer from economic problems and rising crime rates.[[138]](#cite_note-141) Growing fiscal deficits in 1975 led the city to appeal to the federal government for financial aid, which President [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford") denied in a speech paraphrased by *[New York Daily News](/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")* as "[FORD TO CITY: DROP DEAD](/wiki/Ford_to_City:_Drop_Dead "Ford to City: Drop Dead")".[[139]](#cite_note-142) The [Municipal Assistance Corporation](/wiki/Municipal_Assistance_Corporation "Municipal Assistance Corporation") was formed and granted oversight authority over the city's finances.[[140]](#cite_note-143) While a resurgence in the financial industry greatly improved the city's economic health in the 1980s, New York's crime rate continued to increase through that decade and into the beginning of the 1990s.[[141]](#cite_note-144)

New York City's population passed 8 million for the first time in the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census");[[142]](#cite_note-145) further records were set in the [2010](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census") and [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census") censuses.[[143]](#cite_note-146) Important new economic sectors, such as [Silicon Alley](/wiki/Silicon_Alley "Silicon Alley"), emerged.[[144]](#cite_note-Waller2013-147) The year 2000 was celebrated with fanfare in [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square").[[145]](#cite_note-NYC-Y2K-148)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/North_face_south_tower_after_plane_strike_9-11.jpg/250px-North_face_south_tower_after_plane_strike_9-11.jpg)](/wiki/File:North_face_south_tower_after_plane_strike_9-11.jpg)

The [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)"), in [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), during the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") in 2001

New York City suffered the bulk of the [economic damage](/wiki/Economic_effects_of_the_September_11_attacks#New_York_City "Economic effects of the September 11 attacks") and the largest loss of human life in the aftermath of the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") in 2001.[[146]](#cite_note-Dieterle2017-149) Two of the four hijacked airliners were flown into the Twin Towers of the [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)"), resulting in the collapse of both buildings and the deaths of 2,753 people, including 343 first responders from the [New York City Fire Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Fire_Department "New York City Fire Department") and 71 law enforcement officers.[[147]](#cite_note-150)

[The area was rebuilt](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site#Planning_for_the_new_World_Trade_Center "World Trade Center site") with a [new World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(2001%E2%80%93present) "World Trade Center (2001–present)"), the [National September 11 Memorial and Museum](/wiki/National_September_11_Memorial_and_Museum "National September 11 Memorial and Museum"), and other new buildings and infrastructure,[[148]](#cite_note-Greenspan2013-151) including the [World Trade Center Transportation Hub](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_Transportation_Hub "World Trade Center Transportation Hub"), the city's third-largest hub.[[149]](#cite_note-152) The new One World Trade Center is the tallest skyscraper in the Western Hemisphere[[150]](#cite_note-OneWTCtallest-153) and the [world's seventh-tallest building](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_world "List of tallest buildings in the world") by [pinnacle](/wiki/Pinnacle "Pinnacle") height, with its [spire](/wiki/Spire "Spire") reaching a symbolic 1,776 feet (541.3 m), a reference to the year of [American independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence").[[151]](#cite_note-154)[[152]](#cite_note-155)[[153]](#cite_note-156)

The [Occupy Wall Street](/wiki/Occupy_Wall_Street "Occupy Wall Street") protests in [Zuccotti Park](/wiki/Zuccotti_Park "Zuccotti Park") in the [Financial District](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan") of Lower Manhattan began on September 17, 2011, receiving global attention and popularizing the [Occupy movement](/wiki/Occupy_movement "Occupy movement") against [social](/wiki/Social_inequality "Social inequality") and [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality") worldwide.[[154]](#cite_note-157)

New York City was [heavily impacted](/wiki/Effects_of_Hurricane_Sandy_in_New_York "Effects of Hurricane Sandy in New York") by [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") in October 2012. At least 43 people died in New York City as a result of Sandy, and the economic losses in New York City were estimated to be roughly $19 billion.[[155]](#cite_note-158) Flooding led to a days-long shutdown of the subway system,[[156]](#cite_note-159) and the first weather-related closure of the New York Stock Exchange since the [Great Blizzard of 1888](/wiki/Great_Blizzard_of_1888 "Great Blizzard of 1888").[[157]](#cite_note-160) The resulting long-term damage to multiple subway and road tunnels[[158]](#cite_note-161) spawned long-term efforts towards infrastructural projects to counter [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change") and rising seas, including $15 billion in federal funding received through 2022 towards those resiliency efforts.[[159]](#cite_note-ClimateResiliency2-162)[[160]](#cite_note-163)

In March 2020, the first case of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_City "COVID-19 pandemic in New York City") in the city was confirmed.[[161]](#cite_note-164) With its population density and extensive exposure to global travelers, the city rapidly replaced [Wuhan](/wiki/Wuhan "Wuhan"), China as the global epicenter of [the pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") during the early phase, straining the city's healthcare infrastructure.[[162]](#cite_note-165)[[163]](#cite_note-166) Through March 2023, New York City recorded [more than 80,000 deaths](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_City "COVID-19 pandemic in New York City") from COVID-19-related complications.[[164]](#cite_note-167)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of New York City](/wiki/Geography_of_New_York_City "Geography of New York City") and [Geography of New York–New Jersey Harbor Estuary](/wiki/Geography_of_New_York%E2%80%93New_Jersey_Harbor_Estuary "Geography of New York–New Jersey Harbor Estuary")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/Core_of_New_York_City_by_Sentinel-2.jpg/250px-Core_of_New_York_City_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Core_of_New_York_City_by_Sentinel-2.jpg)

Aerial view of the [New York City metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") with Manhattan at its center

New York City lies in the northeastern United States, in southeastern New York State, approximately halfway between Washington, D.C. and Boston. Its location at the mouth of the [Hudson River](/wiki/Hudson_River "Hudson River"), which feeds into a naturally sheltered harbor and then into the Atlantic Ocean, has helped the city become a significant trading port. Most of the city is built on the three islands of Long Island, Manhattan, and Staten Island.

During the [Wisconsin glaciation](/wiki/Wisconsin_glaciation "Wisconsin glaciation"), 75,000 to 11,000 years ago, the New York City area was situated at the edge of a large [ice sheet](/wiki/Ice_sheet "Ice sheet").[[165]](#cite_note-JacksonKeller2010a-168) The erosive forward movement of the ice (and its subsequent retreat) contributed to the separation of what is now Long Island and Staten Island. That action left [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") at a relatively shallow depth, providing a solid [foundation](/wiki/Foundation_(engineering) "Foundation (engineering)") for most of Manhattan's skyscrapers.[[166]](#cite_note-169)

The Hudson River flows through the Hudson Valley into [New York Bay](/wiki/New_York_Bay "New York Bay"). Between New York City and [Troy, New York](/wiki/Troy,_New_York "Troy, New York"), the river is an [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary").[[167]](#cite_note-170) The Hudson River separates the city from New Jersey. The East River—a [tidal strait](/wiki/Tidal_strait "Tidal strait")—flows from [Long Island Sound](/wiki/Long_Island_Sound "Long Island Sound") and separates the Bronx and Manhattan from Long Island. The [Harlem River](/wiki/Harlem_River "Harlem River"), another tidal strait between the East and Hudson rivers, separates most of Manhattan from the Bronx. The [Bronx River](/wiki/Bronx_River "Bronx River"), which flows through the Bronx and [Westchester County](/wiki/Westchester_County "Westchester County"), is the only entirely [freshwater](/wiki/Fresh_water "Fresh water") river in the city.[[168]](#cite_note-nytimes-171)[*[importance?](/wiki/Wikipedia:What_Wikipedia_is_not#Encyclopedic_content "Wikipedia:What Wikipedia is not")*]

The city's land has been altered substantially by human intervention, with considerable [land reclamation](/wiki/Land_reclamation "Land reclamation") along the [waterfronts](/wiki/Waterfront_(area) "Waterfront (area)") since Dutch colonial times; reclamation is most prominent in Lower Manhattan, with developments such as [Battery Park City](/wiki/Battery_Park_City "Battery Park City") in the 1970s and 1980s.[[169]](#cite_note-gillespie-p71-172) Some of the natural relief in topography has been evened out, especially in Manhattan.[[170]](#cite_note-173)

The city's total area is 468.484 square miles (1,213.37 km2). 302.643 sq mi (783.84 km2) of the city is land and 165.841 sq mi (429.53 km2) of it is water.[[171]](#cite_note-CensusGazetteer-174)[[172]](#cite_note-NYT_Land_Estimate-175) The highest point in the city is [Todt Hill](/wiki/Todt_Hill "Todt Hill") on Staten Island, which, at 409.8 feet (124.9 m) [above sea level](/wiki/Above_mean_sea_level "Above mean sea level"), is the highest point on the eastern seaboard south of [Maine](/wiki/Maine "Maine").[[173]](#cite_note-176) The summit of the ridge is mostly covered in [woodlands](/wiki/Woodland "Woodland") as part of the [Staten Island Greenbelt](/wiki/Staten_Island_Greenbelt "Staten Island Greenbelt").[[174]](#cite_note-177)

### Boroughs

Main articles: [Boroughs of New York City](/wiki/Boroughs_of_New_York_City "Boroughs of New York City") and [Neighborhoods in New York City](/wiki/Neighborhoods_in_New_York_City "Neighborhoods in New York City")

[![A map showing five boroughs in different colors.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/5_Boroughs_Labels_New_York_City_Map.svg/250px-5_Boroughs_Labels_New_York_City_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:5_Boroughs_Labels_New_York_City_Map.svg)

1. [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") (New York County)

2. [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") (Kings County)

3. [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") (Queens County)

4. [The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") (Bronx County)

5. [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") (Richmond County)

New York City is sometimes referred to collectively as the *Five Boroughs*.[[175]](#cite_note-178) Each borough is coextensive with a respective [county](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_New_York_(state)#County "Administrative divisions of New York (state)") of New York State, making New York City one of the [U.S. municipalities in multiple counties](/wiki/List_of_U.S._municipalities_in_multiple_counties "List of U.S. municipalities in multiple counties").

[Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") (New York County) is the geographically smallest and most densely populated borough. It is home to Central Park and most of the city's skyscrapers, and is sometimes locally known as *The City*.[[176]](#cite_note-ManhattanTheCity-179) Manhattan's population density of 70,450.8 inhabitants per square mile (27,201.2/km2) in 2022 makes it the [highest of any county in the United States](/wiki/County_statistics_of_the_United_States#Population_density "County statistics of the United States") and [higher than the density of any individual American city](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population_density#New_York_City_boroughs "List of United States cities by population density").[[177]](#cite_note-CensusDensity2022-180) Manhattan is the cultural, administrative, and [financial center](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center") of New York City and contains the headquarters of many major [multinational corporations](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation"), the [United Nations headquarters](/wiki/United_Nations_headquarters "United Nations headquarters"), Wall Street, and many important universities. The borough is often described as the financial and cultural center of the world.[[178]](#cite_note-181)[[179]](#cite_note-182)

[Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") (Kings County), on the western tip of [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), is the city's most populous borough. Brooklyn is known for its cultural, social, and ethnic diversity, an independent art scene, [distinct neighborhoods](/wiki/List_of_Brooklyn_neighborhoods "List of Brooklyn neighborhoods"), and a distinctive architectural heritage. [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn") is the largest central core neighborhood in the Outer Boroughs. The borough has a long beachfront shoreline including [Coney Island](/wiki/Coney_Island "Coney Island"), established in the 1870s as one of the earliest amusement grounds in the United States[[180]](#cite_note-183) [Marine Park](/wiki/Marine_Park_(Brooklyn_park) "Marine Park (Brooklyn park)") and [Prospect Park](/wiki/Prospect_Park_(Brooklyn) "Prospect Park (Brooklyn)") are the two largest parks in Brooklyn.[[181]](#cite_note-184) Since 2010, Brooklyn has evolved into a thriving hub of [entrepreneurship](/wiki/Entrepreneurship "Entrepreneurship") and [high technology](/wiki/High_tech "High tech") [startup firms](/wiki/Startup_company "Startup company"),[[182]](#cite_note-185)[[183]](#cite_note-BrooklynDesignHub-186) and of [postmodern art](/wiki/Postmodern_art "Postmodern art") and design.[[183]](#cite_note-BrooklynDesignHub-186)[[184]](#cite_note-BrooklynArt1-187) Brooklyn is also home to [Fort Hamilton](/wiki/Fort_Hamilton "Fort Hamilton"), the [U.S. military's](/wiki/United_States_Armed_Forces "United States Armed Forces") only active duty installation within New York City,[[185]](#cite_note-188) aside from [Coast Guard](/wiki/U.S._Coast_Guard "U.S. Coast Guard") operations. The facility was established in 1825 on the site of a [battery](/wiki/Artillery_battery "Artillery battery") used during the [American Revolution](/wiki/American_Revolution "American Revolution"), and it is one of America's longest-serving military forts.[[186]](#cite_note-189)

[Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens") (Queens County), on Long Island north and east of [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"), is geographically the largest borough, the most [ethnically diverse](/wiki/Ethnic_diversity "Ethnic diversity") county in the United States,[[187]](#cite_note-queensdiverse-190) and the most ethnically diverse urban area in the world.[[188]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld1-191)[[189]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld2-192) Queens is the site of the [Citi Field](/wiki/Citi_Field "Citi Field"), home of the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets"), and hosts the annual [US Open tennis tournament](/wiki/US_Open_(tennis) "US Open (tennis)") at the [USTA Billie Jean King National Tennis Center](/wiki/USTA_Billie_Jean_King_National_Tennis_Center "USTA Billie Jean King National Tennis Center") in [Flushing Meadows–Corona Park](/wiki/Flushing_Meadows%E2%80%93Corona_Park "Flushing Meadows–Corona Park"), with plans to build [Etihad Park](/wiki/Etihad_Park_(New_York_City) "Etihad Park (New York City)"), a soccer-specific stadium for [New York City FC](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC").[[190]](#cite_note-193) Additionally, two of the three busiest airports serving the New York metropolitan area, [John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") and [LaGuardia Airport](/wiki/LaGuardia_Airport "LaGuardia Airport"), are in Queens.[[191]](#cite_note-194)

[The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") (Bronx County) is both New York City's northernmost borough and the only one that is mostly on the [U.S. mainland](/wiki/U.S._mainland "U.S. mainland"). It is the location of [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium"), the baseball park of the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"), and home to the largest [cooperatively-owned housing](/wiki/Housing_cooperative "Housing cooperative") complex in the United States, [Co-op City](/wiki/Co-op_City,_Bronx "Co-op City, Bronx").[[192]](#cite_note-195) It is home to the [Bronx Zoo](/wiki/Bronx_Zoo "Bronx Zoo"), the world's largest metropolitan zoo,[[193]](#cite_note-BronxZoo-196) which spans 265 acres (1.07 km2) and houses more than 6,000 animals.[[194]](#cite_note-197) The Bronx is the birthplace of [hip hop music](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music") and its associated [culture](/wiki/Hip_hop_culture "Hip hop culture").[[195]](#cite_note-Toop-1992-198) [Pelham Bay Park](/wiki/Pelham_Bay_Park "Pelham Bay Park") is the largest park in New York City, at 2,772 acres (1,122 ha).[[196]](#cite_note-nyt20130601-199)

[Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island") (Richmond County) is the most suburban in character of the five boroughs. It is connected to Brooklyn by the [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge"), and to Manhattan by way of the free [Staten Island Ferry](/wiki/Staten_Island_Ferry "Staten Island Ferry"). Staten Island is known as the "Borough of Parks"[[197]](#cite_note-200) and "The Greenest Borough" due to its 12,300 acres of protected parkland and over 170 parks.[[198]](#cite_note-:0-201) In central Staten Island, the [Staten Island Greenbelt](/wiki/Staten_Island_Greenbelt "Staten Island Greenbelt") spans approximately 2,500 acres (10 km2), including 28 miles (45 km) of walking trails and one of the last undisturbed forests in the city.[[199]](#cite_note-202) Designated in 1984 to protect the island's natural lands, the Greenbelt comprises seven city parks. It also contains environmental centers for educational and recreational purposes.[[198]](#cite_note-:0-201)

### Climate

Main article: [Climate of New York City](/wiki/Climate_of_New_York_City "Climate of New York City")

Under the [Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"), New York City has a [humid subtropical climate](/wiki/Humid_subtropical_climate "Humid subtropical climate") (*Cfa*) and is the northernmost major city on the North American continent with this categorization. The suburbs to the immediate north and west are in the transitional zone between humid subtropical and [humid continental climates](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") (*Dfa*).[[200]](#cite_note-Peel,_M._C.;_Finlayson,_B._L.;_McMahon,_T._A-203)[[201]](#cite_note-newyorkpolonia.com-204) The city receives an average of 49.5 inches (1,260 mm) of precipitation annually, which is relatively evenly spread throughout the year. New York averages [over 2,500 hours of sunshine](/wiki/Climate_of_New_York_City#Other_phenomena "Climate of New York City") annually.[[202]](#cite_note-NYCSunshine-205)

Winters are chilly and damp, and prevailing wind patterns that blow [sea breezes](/wiki/Sea_breeze "Sea breeze") offshore temper the moderating effects of the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"). The Atlantic and the partial shielding from colder air by the [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains") keep the city warmer in the winter than inland North American cities at similar or lesser latitudes.[[203]](#cite_note-206) The daily mean temperature in January, the area's coldest month, is 33.3 °F (0.7 °C).[[204]](#cite_note-NOAA_txt-207) Temperatures usually drop to 10 °F (−12 °C) several times per winter,[[205]](#cite_note-NYC_climate-208) and can reach 60 °F (16 °C) for several days even in the coldest winter month. Spring and autumn are unpredictable and can range from cool to warm, although they are usually mild with low humidity. Summers are typically hot and humid, with a daily mean temperature of 77.5 °F (25.3 °C) in July.[[204]](#cite_note-NOAA_txt-207)

Nighttime temperatures are 9.5 °F (5.3 °C) degrees higher for the average city resident due to the [urban heat island](/wiki/Urban_heat_island "Urban heat island") effect, caused by paved streets and tall buildings.[[206]](#cite_note-209) Daytime temperatures exceed 90 °F (32 °C) on average of 17 days each summer and in some years exceed 100 °F (38 °C), although this is a rare occurrence, last noted on July 18, 2012.[[207]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA-210)[[208]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA_txt-211)[[209]](#cite_note-noaasun-212)[[210]](#cite_note-Weather_Atlas_NYC-213) Readings of 0 °F (−18 °C) are extremely rare, last occurring on February 14, 2016.[[211]](#cite_note-214)

Extreme temperatures have ranged from 106 °F (41 °C), recorded on July 9, 1936, down to −15 °F (−26 °C) on February 9, 1934.[[204]](#cite_note-NOAA_txt-207) The coldest recorded wind chill was −37 °F (−38 °C) on the same day as the all-time record low.[[212]](#cite_note-215) The average winter snowfall between 1991 and 2020 was 29.8 inches (76 cm). This varies considerably between years. The record cold daily maximum was 2 °F (−17 °C) on December 30, 1917. The record warm daily minimum was 87 °F (31 °C), on July 2, 1903.[[207]](#cite_note-New_York_City_Weatherbox_NOAA-210) The average water temperature of the nearby Atlantic Ocean ranges from 39.7 °F (4.3 °C) in February, to 74.1 °F (23.4 °C) in August.[[213]](#cite_note-216)

[Hurricanes](/wiki/Hurricane "Hurricane") and [tropical storms](/wiki/Tropical_storm "Tropical storm") are rare in the New York area.[[214]](#cite_note-217) Hurricane Sandy brought a destructive [storm surge](/wiki/Storm_surge "Storm surge") to New York City on the evening of October 29, 2012, flooding numerous streets, tunnels, and subway lines in Lower Manhattan and other areas of the city and cutting off electricity in many parts of the city and its suburbs.[[215]](#cite_note-218) The storm and its profound impacts have prompted the discussion of constructing [seawalls](/wiki/Seawall "Seawall") and other coastal barriers around the shorelines of the city and the metropolitan area to minimize the risk of destructive consequences from another such event in the future.[[159]](#cite_note-ClimateResiliency2-162)
