### Parks

Main articles: [New York City Department of Parks and Recreation](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Parks_and_Recreation "New York City Department of Parks and Recreation") and [List of New York City parks](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_parks "List of New York City parks")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Statue_of_Liberty%2C_NY.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Liberty%2C_NY.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Liberty,_NY.jpg)

The [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty") on [Liberty Island](/wiki/Liberty_Island "Liberty Island") in [New York Harbor](/wiki/New_York_Harbor "New York Harbor"), a global symbol of the United States and [its ideals of liberty, freedom, and opportunity](/wiki/American_way "American way")[[115]](#cite_note-Statue_of_Liberty_UNESCO-118)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Central_Park_-_The_Pond_%2848377220157%29.jpg/250px-Central_Park_-_The_Pond_%2848377220157%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Central_Park_-_The_Pond_(48377220157).jpg)

[The Pond](/wiki/The_Pond_and_Hallett_Nature_Sanctuary "The Pond and Hallett Nature Sanctuary") and [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") as seen from [Gapstow Bridge](/wiki/Gapstow_Bridge "Gapstow Bridge") in [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park")

The city of New York has a complex park system, with various lands operated by the [National Park Service](/wiki/National_Park_Service "National Park Service"), the [New York State Office of Parks, Recreation and Historic Preservation](/wiki/New_York_State_Office_of_Parks,_Recreation_and_Historic_Preservation "New York State Office of Parks, Recreation and Historic Preservation"), and the [New York City Department of Parks and Recreation](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Parks_and_Recreation "New York City Department of Parks and Recreation"). In its 2023 ParkScore ranking, the [Trust for Public Land](/wiki/Trust_for_Public_Land "Trust for Public Land") reported that the park system in New York City was the tenth-best park system among the most populous U.S. cities, citing the city's park acreage, investment in parks, and that 99% of residents are within 1⁄2 mile (0.80 km) of a park.[[217]](#cite_note-222)

[Gateway National Recreation Area](/wiki/Gateway_National_Recreation_Area "Gateway National Recreation Area") contains over 26,000 acres (110 km2), most of it in New York City.[[218]](#cite_note-223) In Brooklyn and Queens, the park contains over 9,000 acres (36 km2) of [salt marsh](/wiki/Salt_marsh "Salt marsh"), [wetlands](/wiki/Wetland "Wetland"), islands, and water, including most of [Jamaica Bay](/wiki/Jamaica_Bay "Jamaica Bay") and the [Jamaica Bay Wildlife Refuge](/wiki/Jamaica_Bay_Wildlife_Refuge "Jamaica Bay Wildlife Refuge"). Also in Queens, the park includes a significant portion of the western [Rockaway Peninsula](/wiki/Rockaway_Peninsula "Rockaway Peninsula"), most notably [Jacob Riis Park](/wiki/Jacob_Riis_Park "Jacob Riis Park") and [Fort Tilden](/wiki/Fort_Tilden "Fort Tilden").[[219]](#cite_note-224) In Staten Island, it includes [Fort Wadsworth](/wiki/Fort_Wadsworth "Fort Wadsworth"), with historic pre-Civil War era [Battery Weed](/wiki/Battery_Weed "Battery Weed") and [Fort Tompkins](/wiki/Fort_Tompkins_Quadrangle "Fort Tompkins Quadrangle"), and [Great Kills Park](/wiki/Great_Kills_Park "Great Kills Park").[[220]](#cite_note-225)

The [Statue of Liberty National Monument](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty_National_Monument "Statue of Liberty National Monument") and [Ellis Island Immigration Museum](/wiki/Ellis_Island_Immigration_Museum "Ellis Island Immigration Museum") are managed by the National Park Service and are in both New York and New Jersey. They are joined in the harbor by [Governors Island National Monument](/wiki/Governors_Island_National_Monument "Governors Island National Monument"). Historic sites under federal management on Manhattan Island include [Stonewall National Monument](/wiki/Stonewall_National_Monument "Stonewall National Monument"); [Castle Clinton National Monument](/wiki/Castle_Clinton_National_Monument "Castle Clinton National Monument"); Federal Hall National Memorial; [Theodore Roosevelt Birthplace National Historic Site](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt_Birthplace_National_Historic_Site "Theodore Roosevelt Birthplace National Historic Site"); [General Grant National Memorial](/wiki/General_Grant_National_Memorial "General Grant National Memorial") (Grant's Tomb); [African Burial Ground National Monument](/wiki/African_Burial_Ground_National_Monument "African Burial Ground National Monument"); and [Hamilton Grange National Memorial](/wiki/Hamilton_Grange_National_Memorial "Hamilton Grange National Memorial"). [Hundreds of properties](/wiki/List_of_National_Historic_Landmarks_in_New_York_City "List of National Historic Landmarks in New York City") are listed on the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") or as a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark").

There are seven state parks within the confines of New York City. They include: the [Clay Pit Ponds State Park Preserve](/wiki/Clay_Pit_Ponds_State_Park_Preserve "Clay Pit Ponds State Park Preserve"), a natural area that includes extensive [riding trails](/wiki/Trail_riding "Trail riding"); the [Riverbank State Park](/wiki/Riverbank_State_Park "Riverbank State Park"), a 28-acre (11 ha) facility;[[221]](#cite_note-226) and the [Marsha P. Johnson State Park](/wiki/Marsha_P._Johnson_State_Park "Marsha P. Johnson State Park"), a state park in Brooklyn and Manhattan that borders the East River renamed in honor of [Marsha P. Johnson](/wiki/Marsha_P._Johnson "Marsha P. Johnson").[[222]](#cite_note-227)

New York City has over 28,000 acres (110 km2) of [municipal parkland](/wiki/Urban_park "Urban park") and 14 miles (23 km) of public beaches.[[223]](#cite_note-228) The largest municipal park in the city is Pelham Bay Park in the Bronx, with 2,772 acres (1,122 ha),[[196]](#cite_note-nyt20130601-199)[[224]](#cite_note-229) and the most visited urban park is the [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park"), and one of the most filmed and visited locations in the world, with 42 million visitors in 2023.[[225]](#cite_note-NYCvisitors-230)

### Environment

Main article: [Environmental issues in New York City](/wiki/Environmental_issues_in_New_York_City "Environmental issues in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Recycling_combine_-_Sunset_Pk%2C_NYC_05.jpg/250px-Recycling_combine_-_Sunset_Pk%2C_NYC_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Recycling_combine_-_Sunset_Pk,_NYC_05.jpg)

The [Sunset Park Material Recovery Facility](/wiki/Sunset_Park_Material_Recovery_Facility "Sunset Park Material Recovery Facility") is the largest [commingled recycling facility](/wiki/Single-stream_recycling "Single-stream recycling") in the United States.[[226]](#cite_note-231)[[227]](#cite_note-232)

Environmental issues in New York City are affected by the city's size, density, [abundant public transportation infrastructure](/wiki/Transportation_in_New_York_City "Transportation in New York City"), and its location at the mouth of the Hudson River. For example, it is one of the country's biggest sources of pollution and has the lowest per-capita [greenhouse gas emissions](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas_emissions "Greenhouse gas emissions") rate and electricity usage. [Governors Island](/wiki/Governors_Island "Governors Island") is planned to host a US$1 billion research and education center to make New York City the global leader in addressing the [climate crisis](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change").[[228]](#cite_note-NYCGlobalClimateLeader-233)

As an [oceanic port](/wiki/Port "Port") city, New York City is vulnerable to long-term manifestations of [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming") like [sea level rise](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") exacerbated by [land subsidence](/wiki/Land_subsidence "Land subsidence").[[229]](#cite_note-234) Climate change has spawned the development of a significant [climate resiliency](/wiki/Green_economy "Green economy") and [environmental sustainability](/wiki/Environmental_sustainability "Environmental sustainability") economy in the city. New York City has focused on reducing its [environmental impact](/wiki/Human_impact_on_the_environment "Human impact on the environment") and [carbon footprint](/wiki/Carbon_footprint "Carbon footprint").[[230]](#cite_note-NYCCarbonFootprint-235) [Mass transit](/wiki/Mass_transit "Mass transit") use is the highest in the country.

New York's [high rate of public transit use](/wiki/List_of_U.S._cities_with_high_transit_ridership "List of U.S. cities with high transit ridership"), more than 610,000 daily cycling trips as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit),[[231]](#cite_note-NYCcycling-236) and [many pedestrian commuters](/wiki/List_of_U.S._cities_with_most_pedestrian_commuters "List of U.S. cities with most pedestrian commuters") make it the most energy-efficient major city in the United States.[[232]](#cite_note-NYC_energy_consumption-237) Walk and bicycle modes of travel account for 21% of all modes for trips in the city; nationally, the rate for metro regions is about 8%.[[233]](#cite_note-238) In both 2011 and 2015, [Walk Score](/wiki/Walk_Score "Walk Score") named New York City the most [walkable](/wiki/Walkability "Walkability") large city in the United States,[[234]](#cite_note-239)[[235]](#cite_note-240)[[236]](#cite_note-241) and in 2018, *Stacker* ranked New York the most walkable American city.[[237]](#cite_note-242) [Citibank](/wiki/Citibank "Citibank") sponsored public bicycles for the city's [bike-share](/wiki/Bike-share "Bike-share") project, which became known as [Citi Bike](/wiki/Citi_Bike "Citi Bike"), in 2013.[[238]](#cite_note-243) New York City's numerical "in-season cycling indicator" of bicycling in the city had hit an all-time high of 437 when measured in 2014.[[239]](#cite_note-244)

The New York City drinking water supply is extracted from the protected [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains") watershed.[[240]](#cite_note-245) As a result of the watershed's integrity and undisturbed natural [water filtration](/wiki/Water_filtration "Water filtration") system, New York is one of only four major cities in the United States the majority of whose drinking water is pure enough not to require [water treatment](/wiki/Water_treatment "Water treatment").[[241]](#cite_note-246) The city's municipal water system is the nation's largest, moving more than 1 billion U.S. gallons (3.8 billion liters) of water daily from a watershed covering 1,900 square miles (4,900 km2)[[242]](#cite_note-NYTimes-Water-Investment-2018-247)[[243]](#cite_note-248)

According to the 2016 [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") Global Urban Ambient Air Pollution Database,[[244]](#cite_note-249) the annual average concentration in New York City's air of particulate matter measuring 2.5 micrometers or less (PM2.5) was 7.0 micrograms per cubic meter, or 3.0 micrograms within the recommended limit of the WHO Air Quality Guidelines for the annual mean PM2.5.[[245]](#cite_note-250) The [New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Health_and_Mental_Hygiene "New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene"), in partnership with [Queens College](/wiki/Queens_College,_City_University_of_New_York "Queens College, City University of New York"), conducts the New York Community Air Survey to measure pollutants at about 150 locations.[[246]](#cite_note-251)

New York City is the most populous city in the United States, with 8,804,190 residents as of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), its highest decennial count ever, incorporating more immigration into the city than outmigration since the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census").[[6]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-7)[[250]](#cite_note-256)[[251]](#cite_note-nytimes_a-257) More than twice as many people live in New York City as in Los Angeles, the second largest American city.[[252]](#cite_note-estimate2023a-258) The city's population in 2020 was 35.9% [White](/wiki/White_Americans "White Americans"), 22.7% [Black](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), 14.6% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"), 10.5% [Mixed](/wiki/Multiracial_Americans "Multiracial Americans"), 0.7% Native American and 0.1% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans"); 28.4% identified themselves as [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans").[[6]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-7)

Between 2010 and 2020, New York City's population grew by 629,000 residents, more than the total growth of the next four largest American cities (Los Angeles, Chicago, [Houston](/wiki/Houston "Houston"), and [Phoenix](/wiki/Phoenix,_Arizona "Phoenix, Arizona")) combined.[[253]](#cite_note-2010to2020CensusGrowth1-259)[[254]](#cite_note-2010to2020CensusGrowth2-260) The city's population density of 27,744.1 inhabitants per square mile (10,712.1/km2) makes it the densest of any American municipality with a population above 100,000.[[177]](#cite_note-CensusDensity2022-180) Manhattan's population density is 70,450.8 inhabitants per square mile (27,201.2/km2), the highest of any county in the United States.[[177]](#cite_note-CensusDensity2022-180)

Based on data from the 2020 census, New York City comprised about 43.6% of the state's population of 20,202,320,[[6]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-7) and about 39% of the population of the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area").[[255]](#cite_note-PopHousingEstMetro-261) The majority of New York City residents in 2020 (5,141,539 or 58.4%) were living in Brooklyn or Queens, the two boroughs on Long Island.[[256]](#cite_note-QuickFactsFiveBoroughs-262) An estimated 800 languages are spoken in New York,[[26]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sMostCulturallyLinguisticallyDiverseCityTop-29)[[28]](#cite_note-QueensMostLinguisticallyDiverseTop-31)[[257]](#cite_note-263)[[258]](#cite_note-800source3-264) and the New York City metropolitan statistical area has the largest [foreign-born population of any metropolitan region](/wiki/Foreign_born#Metropolitan_regions_with_largest_foreign_born_populations "Foreign born") in the world. The New York region continues to be by far the leading metropolitan gateway for legal immigrants admitted into the United States, substantially exceeding the combined totals of Los Angeles and [Miami](/wiki/Miami_metropolitan_area "Miami metropolitan area").[[259]](#cite_note-Immigrants2022est-265) Nearly seven times as many young professionals applied for jobs in New York City in 2023 as compared to 2019, making New York the most popular destination for recent college graduates.[[260]](#cite_note-NYCMostPopularDestinationCollegeGrads-266)

### Ethnicity and nationality

Main articles: [Race and ethnicity in New York City](/wiki/Race_and_ethnicity_in_New_York_City "Race and ethnicity in New York City") and [New York City ethnic enclaves](/wiki/New_York_City_ethnic_enclaves "New York City ethnic enclaves")

According to 2022 estimates from the [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), the largest self-reported ancestries in New York City were [Dominican](/wiki/Dominican_Americans "Dominican Americans") (8.7%), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Americans "Chinese Americans") (7.5%), [Puerto Rican](/wiki/Puerto_Ricans "Puerto Ricans") (6.9%), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Americans "Italian Americans") (5.5%), [Mexican](/wiki/Mexican_Americans "Mexican Americans") (4.4%), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Americans "Irish Americans") (4.4%), [Asian Indian](/wiki/Indian_Americans "Indian Americans") (3.1%), [German](/wiki/German_Americans "German Americans") (2.9%), [Jamaican](/wiki/Jamaican_Americans "Jamaican Americans") (2.4%), [Ecuadorian](/wiki/Ecuadorian_Americans "Ecuadorian Americans") (2.3%), [English](/wiki/English_Americans "English Americans") (2.1%), [Polish](/wiki/Polish_Americans "Polish Americans") (1.9%), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_Americans "Russian Americans") (1.7%), [Arab](/wiki/Arab_Americans "Arab Americans") (1.4%), [Haitian](/wiki/Haitian_Americans "Haitian Americans") (1.4%), [Guyanese](/wiki/Guyanese_Americans "Guyanese Americans") (1.3%), [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_Americans "Filipino Americans") (1.1%), and [Korean](/wiki/Korean_Americans "Korean Americans") (1.1%).[[261]](#cite_note-United_States_Census_Bureau-267)[[17]](#cite_note-The_City_of_New_York-19)[[18]](#cite_note-Digital_Diplomacy_Coalition,_New_York-20)

Based on American Community Survey data from 2018 to 2022, approximately 36.3% of the city's population is [foreign-born](/wiki/Foreign-born "Foreign-born") (compared to 13.7% nationwide),[[6]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-7) and 40% of all children are born to mothers who are immigrants.[[264]](#cite_note-270) Throughout its history, New York has been a major [port of entry](/wiki/Port_of_entry "Port of entry") for immigrants.[[265]](#cite_note-CityDiversity-271)[[266]](#cite_note-NYC_immigration-272) No single country or region of origin dominates.[[265]](#cite_note-CityDiversity-271) Queens has the largest Asian-American and [Andean populations](/wiki/Andean_civilizations "Andean civilizations") in the United States, and is also the most ethnically and linguistically diverse urban area in the world.[[267]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld3-273)[[189]](#cite_note-QueensMostDiverseWorld2-192) The wider New York City metropolitan region is home to the world's largest [foreign-born population of any metropolitan region](/wiki/Foreign_born#Metropolitan_and_urban_regions_with_the_largest_foreign-born_populations "Foreign born"), enumerating 5.9 million as of 2023.

The metropolitan area has the largest [Asian Indian](/wiki/Asian_Indian "Asian Indian") population in the Western Hemisphere; the largest [Russian-American](/wiki/Russian_Americans "Russian Americans"),[[268]](#cite_note-U.S._Department_of_Homeland_Security-274) Italian-American, and African-American populations; the largest [Dominican-American](/wiki/Dominican-American "Dominican-American"), [Puerto Rican–American](/wiki/Puerto_Rican_migration_to_New_York_City "Puerto Rican migration to New York City"), and [South American](/wiki/South_American_Americans "South American Americans")[[268]](#cite_note-U.S._Department_of_Homeland_Security-274) and second-largest overall [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_American "Hispanic and Latino American") population in the United States, numbering over 5 million. [Venezuela](/wiki/Venezuelan_Americans "Venezuelan Americans"), [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), [Colombia](/wiki/Colombian_Americans "Colombian Americans"), [Guyana](/wiki/Guyanese_Americans "Guyanese Americans"), [Peru](/wiki/Peruvian_Americans "Peruvian Americans"), and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazilian_Americans "Brazilian Americans"), are the top source countries from South America for immigrants to the New York City region; the [Dominican Republic](/wiki/Dominican_Republic "Dominican Republic"), [Jamaica](/wiki/Jamaica "Jamaica"), [Haiti](/wiki/Haiti "Haiti"), and [Trinidad and Tobago](/wiki/Trinidadian_and_Tobagonian_Americans "Trinidadian and Tobagonian Americans") in the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbeans_in_New_York_City "Caribbeans in New York City"); [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigerian_Americans "Nigerian Americans"), [Egypt](/wiki/Egyptian_Americans "Egyptian Americans"), [Ghana](/wiki/Ghanaian_Americans "Ghanaian Americans"), [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzanian_Americans "Tanzanian Americans"), [Kenya](/wiki/Kenyan_Americans "Kenyan Americans"), and [South Africa](/wiki/South_African_Americans "South African Americans") from [Africa](/wiki/African_immigration_to_the_United_States "African immigration to the United States"); and [El Salvador](/wiki/Salvadoran_Americans "Salvadoran Americans"), [Honduras](/wiki/Honduran_Americans "Honduran Americans"), and [Guatemala](/wiki/Guatemalan_Americans "Guatemalan Americans") in Central America.[[269]](#cite_note-275)

New York contains the highest total Asian population of any U.S. city proper.[[270]](#cite_note-276) [Asian Americans in New York City](/wiki/Asian_Americans_in_New_York_City "Asian Americans in New York City"), according to the 2010 census, number more than 1.2 million,[[6]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-7) greater than the combined totals of San Francisco and Los Angeles.[[271]](#cite_note-277) New York has the largest [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_people_in_New_York_City "Chinese people in New York City") population of any city outside Asia,[[272]](#cite_note-NYCLargestChinesePopulation-278) [Manhattan's Chinatown](/wiki/Chinatown,_Manhattan "Chinatown, Manhattan") is the highest concentration of Chinese people in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere"),[[273]](#cite_note-fact-sheet-279) and Queens is home to the largest [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibetan_people "Tibetan people") population outside Asia.[[274]](#cite_note-UnreachedNY-280) [Arab Americans](/wiki/Arab_Americans "Arab Americans") number over 160,000 in New York City,[[275]](#cite_note-281) with the highest concentration in Brooklyn. New York City has the highest [Palestinian](/wiki/Palestinian_Americans "Palestinian Americans") population in the United States.[[276]](#cite_note-NYCLargestPalestinianPopulation-282) [Uzbek Americans](/wiki/Uzbek_American "Uzbek American"), [Armenian Americans](/wiki/Armenian_Americans "Armenian Americans"), and [Albanian Americans](/wiki/Albanian_Americans "Albanian Americans") are a rapidly growing segment of the city's non-Hispanic White population.[[277]](#cite_note-283)[[278]](#cite_note-284) The metropolitan area is home to 20% of the nation's [Indian Americans](/wiki/Indians_in_the_New_York_City_metropolitan_region "Indians in the New York City metropolitan region") and at least twenty [Little India](/wiki/Little_India_(location) "Little India (location)") enclaves, and 15% of all [Korean Americans](/wiki/Korean_Americans_in_New_York_City "Korean Americans in New York City") and four [Koreatowns](/wiki/Koreatown,_Manhattan "Koreatown, Manhattan").[[279]](#cite_note-NYCAmericanCommunitySurvey-285) New York City and its metropolitan area have the largest population of [Blacks or African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans_in_New_York_City "African Americans in New York City") in the nation.[[280]](#cite_note-286)

New York City has the largest [European](/wiki/European_American "European American") and [non-Hispanic white](/wiki/Non-Hispanic_whites "Non-Hispanic whites") population of any American city, numbering 2.7 million in 2012.[[281]](#cite_note-287) The [European diaspora](/wiki/European_diaspora "European diaspora") residing in the city is very diverse and many [European ethnic groups have formed enclaves](/wiki/New_York_City_ethnic_enclaves#European "New York City ethnic enclaves").[[282]](#cite_note-BrightonBeachRussianSpeakingEpicenter-288)[[283]](#cite_note-AstoriaNYCGreektown1-289)[[284]](#cite_note-AstoriaNYCGreektown2-290) With 960,000 Jewish inhabitants as of 2023, New York City is home to the highest [Jewish population](/wiki/Jewish_population_by_city "Jewish population by city") of any city in the world,[[285]](#cite_note-Gergely-2024-291) and its metropolitan area concentrated over 2 million Jews as of 2021, the second largest Jewish population worldwide after the [Tel Aviv metropolitan area](/wiki/Tel_Aviv_metropolitan_area "Tel Aviv metropolitan area") in Israel.[[286]](#cite_note-292) In the borough of Brooklyn, an estimated one in four residents was Jewish as of 2018.[[287]](#cite_note-BrookynTheMostJewishSpotOnEarth-293)

### Sexual orientation and gender identity

Main articles: [LGBTQ culture in New York City](/wiki/LGBTQ_culture_in_New_York_City "LGBTQ culture in New York City"), [Transgender culture of New York City](/wiki/Transgender_culture_of_New_York_City "Transgender culture of New York City"), [Same-sex marriage in New York](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_New_York "Same-sex marriage in New York"), and [NYC Pride March](/wiki/NYC_Pride_March "NYC Pride March")

Further information: [New York City Drag March](/wiki/New_York_City_Drag_March "New York City Drag March"), [Queens Pride Parade](/wiki/Queens_Pride_Parade "Queens Pride Parade"), [List of LGBT people from New York City](/wiki/List_of_LGBT_people_from_New_York_City "List of LGBT people from New York City"), and [List of largest LGBT events](/wiki/List_of_largest_LGBT_events "List of largest LGBT events")

New York City has been described as the [gay capital](/wiki/LGBT_culture_in_New_York_City "LGBT culture in New York City") of the world and the central node of the [lesbian, gay, bisexual, and transgender](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") (LGBT) [sociopolitical ecosystem](/wiki/Political_sociology "Political sociology"), and is home to one of the world's largest LGBT populations and the most prominent.[[288]](#cite_note-NYCGayCapitalOfTheWorld1-294) The New York metropolitan area is home to about 570,000 self-identifying [gay](/wiki/Gays_in_New_York_City "Gays in New York City") and [bisexual](/wiki/Bisexuality "Bisexuality") people, [the largest in the country](/wiki/LGBT_demographics_of_the_United_States#By_metropolitan_area "LGBT demographics of the United States").[[289]](#cite_note-295)[[290]](#cite_note-296) Same-sex sexual activity between consenting adults has been legal in New York since 1980's *[New York v. Onofre](/wiki/New_York_v._Onofre "New York v. Onofre")* case, which invalidated the state's [sodomy law](/wiki/Sodomy_laws_in_the_United_States#State_and_territorial_laws_prior_to_Lawrence_v._Texas "Sodomy laws in the United States").[[291]](#cite_note-297) [Same-sex marriage in New York](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_New_York "Same-sex marriage in New York") was legalized on June 24, 2011, and were authorized to take place on July 23, 2011.[[292]](#cite_note-298)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/NYC_Pride_2019_26.jpg/250px-NYC_Pride_2019_26.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_Pride_2019_26.jpg)

The [NYC Pride March](/wiki/NYC_Pride_March "NYC Pride March") is the [largest pride parade in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_LGBT_events "List of largest LGBT events").[[293]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sLargestPrideParade-299)

The annual [NYC Pride March](/wiki/NYC_Pride_March "NYC Pride March") proceeds southward down [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") and ends at [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village,_Manhattan "Greenwich Village, Manhattan") in Lower Manhattan; the parade is the [largest pride parade in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_LGBT_events "List of largest LGBT events"), attracting tens of thousands of participants and millions of sidewalk spectators each June.[[293]](#cite_note-NYCWorld'sLargestPrideParade-299)[[294]](#cite_note-NYCWorld-300) The annual [Queens Pride Parade](/wiki/Queens_Pride_Parade "Queens Pride Parade") is held in [Jackson Heights](/wiki/Jackson_Heights,_Queens "Jackson Heights, Queens") and is accompanied by the ensuing *Multicultural Parade*.[[295]](#cite_note-NYCTrans-301)

[Stonewall 50 – WorldPride NYC 2019](/wiki/Stonewall_50_%E2%80%93_WorldPride_NYC_2019 "Stonewall 50 – WorldPride NYC 2019") was the [largest international Pride celebration](/wiki/List_of_largest_LGBT_events "List of largest LGBT events") in history, commemorating the 50th anniversary of the Stonewall uprising, with 150,000 participants and five million spectators attending in Manhattan alone.[[296]](#cite_note-Authorities1-302) New York City is home to the largest [transgender](/wiki/Transgender "Transgender") population in the world, estimated at more than 50,000 in 2018, concentrated in Manhattan, Brooklyn, and Queens; however, until the June 1969 Stonewall riots, this community had felt marginalized and neglected by the gay community.[[295]](#cite_note-NYCTrans-301)[[137]](#cite_note-TransEqualityNYC-140) Brooklyn Liberation March, the largest [transgender-rights](/wiki/Transgender_rights "Transgender rights") demonstration in LGBT history, took place on June 14, 2020, stretching from [Grand Army Plaza](/wiki/Grand_Army_Plaza "Grand Army Plaza") to [Fort Greene, Brooklyn](/wiki/Fort_Greene,_Brooklyn "Fort Greene, Brooklyn"), focused on supporting Black transgender lives, drawing an estimated 15,000 to 20,000 participants.[[297]](#cite_note-303)[[298]](#cite_note-304)

### Religion

Further information: [St. Patrick's Cathedral (Midtown Manhattan)](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Cathedral_(Midtown_Manhattan) "St. Patrick's Cathedral (Midtown Manhattan)"), [Rockefeller Center Christmas Tree](/wiki/Rockefeller_Center_Christmas_Tree "Rockefeller Center Christmas Tree"), [History of the Jews in New York City](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_New_York_City "History of the Jews in New York City"), [Jewish arrival in New Amsterdam](/wiki/Jewish_arrival_in_New_Amsterdam "Jewish arrival in New Amsterdam"), [Islam in New York City](/wiki/Islam_in_New_York_City "Islam in New York City"), [Hindu Temple Society of North America](/wiki/Hindu_Temple_Society_of_North_America "Hindu Temple Society of North America"), [Mahayana Buddhism North America](/wiki/Mahayana_Buddhism_North_America "Mahayana Buddhism North America"), and [Falun Gong](/wiki/Falun_Gong "Falun Gong")

Notable religious buildings in New York City

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Emanu-elNYjeh.JPG/250px-Emanu-elNYjeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Emanu-elNYjeh.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/At_New_York%2C_USA_2017_119.jpg/120px-At_New_York%2C_USA_2017_119.jpg)](/wiki/File:At_New_York,_USA_2017_119.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Islamic_Cultural_Center_E96_jeh.JPG/250px-Islamic_Cultural_Center_E96_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Islamic_Cultural_Center_E96_jeh.JPG)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Hindu_Temple_Society_of_North_America_%28Flushing%2C_Queens_-_exterior%29.jpg/120px-Hindu_Temple_Society_of_North_America_%28Flushing%2C_Queens_-_exterior%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hindu_Temple_Society_of_North_America_(Flushing,_Queens_-_exterior).jpg)

Left to right from the top: The [Temple Emanu-El](/wiki/Temple_Emanu-El_of_New_York_(1930) "Temple Emanu-El of New York (1930)"); [St. Patrick's Cathedral](/wiki/St._Patrick%27s_Cathedral_(New_York_City) "St. Patrick's Cathedral (New York City)"); [Islamic Cultural Center of New York](/wiki/Islamic_Cultural_Center_of_New_York "Islamic Cultural Center of New York"); and [Hindu Temple Society of North America](/wiki/Hindu_Temple_Society_of_North_America "Hindu Temple Society of North America")

[Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_United_States "Christianity in the United States") is the largest religion (59% adherent) in New York City,[[299]](#cite_note-NYCReligion1-305) which is home to the highest number of churches of any city in the world.[[20]](#cite_note-NYCTheCapitaloftheWorld-22) [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") is the largest Christian denomination (33%), followed by [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") (23%), and [other Christian denominations](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations "List of Christian denominations") (3%). The [Latin Catholic](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") population is primarily served by the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_New_York "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York") and [Diocese of Brooklyn](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Diocese_of_Brooklyn "Roman Catholic Diocese of Brooklyn"), while [Eastern Catholics](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") are divided into numerous jurisdictions throughout the city. [Evangelical Protestantism](/wiki/Evangelicalism "Evangelicalism") is the largest branch of Protestantism in the city (9%), followed by [Mainline Protestantism](/wiki/Mainline_Protestant "Mainline Protestant") (8%), while the converse is usually true for other U.S. cities and metropolitan areas.[[300]](#cite_note-NYCReligion2-306)

With [960,000 Jewish inhabitants](/wiki/Jews_in_New_York_City "Jews in New York City") as of 2023, [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") is the second-largest religion practiced in New York City,[[285]](#cite_note-Gergely-2024-291) which is home to the largest Jewish population outside of [Tel Aviv](/wiki/Tel_Aviv "Tel Aviv"). Nearly half of the city's Jewish population lives in Brooklyn.[[301]](#cite_note-BrooklynJewish-307)[[287]](#cite_note-BrookynTheMostJewishSpotOnEarth-293)

[Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam") ranks as the third-largest religion in New York City, following Christianity and Judaism, with estimates ranging between 600,000 and 1,000,000 observers of Islam, including 10% of the city's public school children.[[302]](#cite_note-308) 22.3% of [American Muslims](/wiki/Islam_in_the_United_States "Islam in the United States") live in New York City, with 1.5 million Muslims in the greater [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area"), the largest metropolitan Muslim population in the [Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Western_Hemisphere "Western Hemisphere")[[303]](#cite_note-309)—and the most [ethnically diverse](/wiki/Ethnically_diverse "Ethnically diverse") Muslim population of any city in the world.[[304]](#cite_note-NYCMostDiverseMuslimPopulationWorld-310) [Powers Street Mosque](/wiki/Powers_Street_Mosque "Powers Street Mosque") in Brooklyn is one of the oldest continuously operating mosques in the United States, and represents the first Islamic organization in both the city and the state.[[305]](#cite_note-311)[[306]](#cite_note-312)

Following these three largest religious groups in New York City are [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism"), [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"), and others. As of 2023, 24% of Greater New Yorkers identified with no organized religious affiliation, and 4% were self-identified [atheists](/wiki/Atheist "Atheist").[[307]](#cite_note-NYCReligion3-313)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of New York City](/wiki/Economy_of_New_York_City "Economy of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Midtown_Manhattan_from_Weehawken_September_2021_001.jpg/250px-Midtown_Manhattan_from_Weehawken_September_2021_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Midtown_Manhattan_from_Weehawken_September_2021_001.jpg)

[Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") is the world's largest central business district.[[34]](#cite_note-reuters-2022-03-24-37)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/Luchtfoto_van_Lower_Manhattan.jpg/250px-Luchtfoto_van_Lower_Manhattan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Luchtfoto_van_Lower_Manhattan.jpg)

[Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan"), including [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street"), the world's principal financial center,[[33]](#cite_note-NYCFinancialAndFintechCapitalWorld-36) and [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center "One World Trade Center"), the [tallest skyscraper in the United States](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_the_United_States "List of tallest buildings in the United States")

New York City is a global hub of business and commerce, sometimes called the "Capital of the World".[[308]](#cite_note-NewYorkCapitaloftheWorld1-314) Greater New York is the [world's largest metropolitan economy](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_GDP "List of cities by GDP"), with a [gross metropolitan product](/wiki/Gross_metropolitan_product "Gross metropolitan product") estimated at US$2.16 trillion in 2022.[[11]](#cite_note-bea.gov-12)[[12]](#cite_note-fred.stlouisfed.org-13) New York is a center for worldwide banking and finance, health care, and [life sciences](/wiki/Life_sciences "Life sciences"),[[309]](#cite_note-NYCHealthCareLifeSciences-315) [medical technology](/wiki/Medical_technology "Medical technology") and research, retailing, world trade, transportation, tourism, real estate, [new media](/wiki/New_media "New media"), [traditional media](/wiki/Traditional_media "Traditional media"), advertising, [legal services](/wiki/Legal_services "Legal services"), [accountancy](/wiki/Accountancy "Accountancy"), insurance, and the arts in the United States; while [Silicon Alley](/wiki/Silicon_Alley "Silicon Alley"), [metonymous](/wiki/Metonymy "Metonymy") for New York's high technology sphere, continues to expand. The [Port of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port of New York and New Jersey") is a major economic engine, benefitting [post-Panamax](/wiki/Panamax "Panamax") from the expansion of the [Panama Canal](/wiki/Panama_Canal "Panama Canal").[[310]](#cite_note-316)[[311]](#cite_note-317)[[312]](#cite_note-318)

Many [*Fortune* 500](/wiki/Fortune_500 "Fortune 500") corporations are headquartered in New York City,[[313]](#cite_note-319) as are a large number of [multinational corporations](/wiki/Multinational_corporation "Multinational corporation"). New York City has been ranked first among cities across the globe in attracting [capital](/wiki/Capital_(economics) "Capital (economics)"), business, and tourists.[[314]](#cite_note-320)[[315]](#cite_note-321) New York City's role as the top global center for the [advertising industry](/wiki/Advertising "Advertising") is metonymously reflected as *[Madison Avenue](/wiki/Madison_Avenue#Advertising_industry "Madison Avenue")*.[[316]](#cite_note-MadisonAveMetonym-322) The city's [fashion industry](/wiki/Fashion_industry "Fashion industry") provides approximately 180,000 employees with $11 billion in annual wages.[[317]](#cite_note-323)

Significant other economic sectors include universities and non-profit institutions. [Manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing_in_the_United_States "Manufacturing in the United States") declined over the 20th century but still accounts for significant employment. The city's apparel and [garment industry](/wiki/Garment_industry "Garment industry"), historically centered on the [Garment District](/wiki/Garment_District,_Manhattan "Garment District, Manhattan") in Manhattan, peaked in 1950, when more than 323,000 workers were employed in the industry in New York. In 2015, fewer than 23,000 New York City residents were employed in the industry, although revival efforts were underway,[[318]](#cite_note-324) and the American fashion industry continues to be metonymized as *[Seventh Avenue](/wiki/Seventh_Avenue_(Manhattan) "Seventh Avenue (Manhattan)")*.[[319]](#cite_note-325) In 2017, the city had 205,592 employer firms, of which 22.0% were owned by women, 31.3% were minority-owned and 2.7% were owned by veterans.[[6]](#cite_note-QuickFacts-7)

In 2022, the gross domestic product of New York City was US$1.053 trillion, of which $781 billion (74%) was produced by Manhattan.[[11]](#cite_note-bea.gov-12) Like other large cities, New York City has a degree of [income disparity](/wiki/Income_disparity "Income disparity"), as indicated by its [Gini coefficient](/wiki/Gini_coefficient "Gini coefficient") of 0.55 as of 2022.[[320]](#cite_note-326)[[321]](#cite_note-327) In November 2023, the city had total employment of over 4.75 million of which more than a quarter were in education and health services.[[322]](#cite_note-328) Manhattan, which accounted for more than half of the city's jobs, had an average weekly wage of $2,590 in the second quarter of 2023, ranking fourth-highest among the nation's 360 largest counties.[[323]](#cite_note-BLS2023Q3-329) New York City is one of the relatively few American cities levying an [income tax](/wiki/Income_tax "Income tax") (about 3%) on its residents;[[324]](#cite_note-Localities_with_INCOME_tax-330)[[325]](#cite_note-NYCTax-331)[[326]](#cite_note-NYC_tax_schedule-332) despite this tax levy, New York City in 2024 was home by a significant margin to the highest number of billionaires of any city in the world, with a total of 110.[[41]](#cite_note-NYCHighestNumberBillionaires-44)

### Wall Street

Main articles: [Wall Street](/wiki/Wall_Street "Wall Street") and [Financial District, Manhattan](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan")

[![A large flag is stretched over Roman-style columns on the front of a large building.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Gaming-Wall-Street_BTS_Prodigium-266.jpg/250px-Gaming-Wall-Street_BTS_Prodigium-266.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gaming-Wall-Street_BTS_Prodigium-266.jpg)

The [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") is the [world's largest stock exchange](/wiki/List_of_stock_exchanges#Major_stock_exchanges "List of stock exchanges") per total [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of [its listed companies](/wiki/Lists_of_companies_listed_on_the_New_York_Stock_Exchange "Lists of companies listed on the New York Stock Exchange").[[327]](#cite_note-NYSElargest-333)[[328]](#cite_note-NYSEhighestcap-334)

New York City's most important [economic sector](/wiki/Economic_sector "Economic sector") lies in its role as a comprehensive [financial center](/wiki/Financial_center "Financial center"), metonymously known as *Wall Street*. [Lower Manhattan](/wiki/Lower_Manhattan "Lower Manhattan") is home to the [New York Stock Exchange](/wiki/New_York_Stock_Exchange "New York Stock Exchange") and the [Nasdaq](/wiki/Nasdaq "Nasdaq"), representing the world's largest and second largest [stock exchanges](/wiki/Stock_exchange "Stock exchange"), respectively, when measured both by overall average daily trading volume and by total [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization") of their listed companies in 2013.[[327]](#cite_note-NYSElargest-333)[[328]](#cite_note-NYSEhighestcap-334) In fiscal year 2013–14, Wall Street's [securities industry](/wiki/Securities_industry "Securities industry") generated 19% of New York State's tax revenue.[[329]](#cite_note-335)

New York City remains the largest global center for trading in [public equity](/wiki/Public_equity "Public equity") and [debt](/wiki/Security_(finance) "Security (finance)") capital markets.[[330]](#cite_note-CNY2007-336): 31–32[[331]](#cite_note-337) New York also leads in [hedge fund](/wiki/Hedge_fund "Hedge fund") management; [private equity](/wiki/List_of_private_equity_firms "List of private equity firms"); and the monetary volume of [mergers and acquisitions](/wiki/Mergers_and_acquisitions "Mergers and acquisitions"). Several [investment banks](/wiki/Investment_Banking "Investment Banking") and [investment managers](/wiki/Investment_management "Investment management") headquartered in Manhattan are important participants in other global financial centers.[[330]](#cite_note-CNY2007-336): 34–35 New York is the principal [commercial banking](/wiki/Commercial_bank "Commercial bank") center of the United States.[[332]](#cite_note-338)

Manhattan contained over 500 million square feet (46.5 million m2) of office space in 2018,[[333]](#cite_note-ManhattanOfficeSpace-339) making New York City the largest office market in the world,[[334]](#cite_note-340)[[335]](#cite_note-341) while [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), with 400 million square feet (37.2 million m2) in 2018,[[333]](#cite_note-ManhattanOfficeSpace-339) is the largest central business district in the world.[[336]](#cite_note-342)

### Tech and biotech

Further information: [Tech:NYC](/wiki/Tech:NYC "Tech:NYC"), [Tech companies in New York City](/wiki/Tech_companies_in_New_York_City "Tech companies in New York City"), [Biotech companies in New York City](/wiki/Biotech_companies_in_New_York_City "Biotech companies in New York City"), and [Silicon Alley](/wiki/Silicon_Alley "Silicon Alley")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Flatiron_District.jpg/250px-Flatiron_District.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flatiron_District.jpg)

The [Flatiron District](/wiki/Flatiron_District "Flatiron District") is the cradle of Silicon Alley, initially metonymous for the New York metropolitan region's high tech sector.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Cornell_Tech_buildings_%2841991%29.jpg/250px-Cornell_Tech_buildings_%2841991%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cornell_Tech_buildings_(41991).jpg)

[Cornell Tech](/wiki/Cornell_Tech "Cornell Tech") on [Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island")

New York is a top-tier global technology hub.[[15]](#cite_note-NewYorkCityDestinationNumberOneTechHub-17)[[337]](#cite_note-NYCTopTierTechHub-343) Silicon Alley, once a metonym for the sphere encompassing the metropolitan region's [high technology](/wiki/High_tech "High tech") industries,[[338]](#cite_note-Dickey2013-344) is no longer a relevant moniker as the city's tech environment has expanded dramatically both in location and in scope since at least 2003, when tech business appeared in more places in Manhattan and other boroughs, and not much [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon") was involved.[[338]](#cite_note-Dickey2013-344)[[339]](#cite_note-Ward2018-345) New York City's current tech sphere encompasses the array of applications involving universal applications of [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") (AI),[[340]](#cite_note-AI-NYC1A-346)[[341]](#cite_note-AI-NYC1B-347) broadband internet,[[342]](#cite_note-348) [new media](/wiki/New_media "New media"), [financial technology](/wiki/Financial_technology "Financial technology") (*fintech*) and [cryptocurrency](/wiki/Cryptocurrency "Cryptocurrency"), [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotechnology "Biotechnology"), [game design](/wiki/Game_design "Game design"), and other fields within information technology that are supported by its [entrepreneurship ecosystem](/wiki/Entrepreneurship_ecosystem "Entrepreneurship ecosystem") and [venture capital](/wiki/Venture_capital "Venture capital") investments. Technology-driven [startup companies](/wiki/Startup_companies "Startup companies") and entrepreneurial employment are growing in New York City and the region. The technology sector has been claiming a greater share of New York City's economy since 2010.[[343]](#cite_note-TechNYC1-349) [Tech:NYC](/wiki/Tech:NYC "Tech:NYC"), founded in 2016, is a non-profit organization which represents New York City's technology industry with government, civic institutions, in business, and in the media, and whose primary goals are to further augment New York's substantial tech talent base and to advocate for policies that will nurture tech companies to grow in the city.[[344]](#cite_note-350)

New York City's AI sector raised US$483.6 million in venture capital investment in 2022.[[345]](#cite_note-NYCArtificialIntelligenceHub-351) In 2023, New York unveiled the first comprehensive initiative to create both a framework of rules and a [chatbot](/wiki/Chatbot "Chatbot") to regulate the use of AI within the sphere of city government.[[346]](#cite_note-NYCArtificialIntelligenceGovernment-352)

The [biotechnology](/wiki/Biotech_and_pharmaceutical_companies_in_the_New_Jersey/New_York_metropolitan_region "Biotech and pharmaceutical companies in the New Jersey/New York metropolitan region") sector is growing in New York City, based on the city's strength in academic [scientific research](/wiki/Scientific_research "Scientific research") and public and commercial financial support. On December 19, 2011, Mayor Michael R. Bloomberg announced his choice of [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University") and [Technion-Israel Institute of Technology](/wiki/Technion-Israel_Institute_of_Technology "Technion-Israel Institute of Technology") to build a $2 billion graduate school of [applied sciences](/wiki/Applied_science "Applied science") called [Cornell Tech](/wiki/Cornell_Tech "Cornell Tech") on [Roosevelt Island](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island "Roosevelt Island") intending to transform New York City into the world's premier technology capital.[[347]](#cite_note-353)[[348]](#cite_note-354)

### Real estate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/5th_Avenue_9304.JPG/250px-5th_Avenue_9304.JPG)](/wiki/File:5th_Avenue_9304.JPG)

[Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan") is the most expensive shopping street in the world.[[40]](#cite_note-FifthAvenueMostExpensiveStreetOnEarth-43)

New York City real estate is a haven for global investors.[[37]](#cite_note-NYCSafeHavenGlobalInvestors-40) The total value of all New York City property was assessed at US$1.479 trillion for the 2017 [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year"), an increase of 6.1% from the previous year. Of the total market value, single family homes accounted for $765 billion (51.7%); [condominiums](/wiki/Condominium "Condominium"), [co-ops](/wiki/Housing_cooperative "Housing cooperative"), and apartment buildings totaled $351 billion (23.7%); and commercial properties were valued at $317 billion (21.4%).[[349]](#cite_note-355)[[350]](#cite_note-NYC_real_estate-356) [Fifth Avenue](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") in Midtown Manhattan commands the highest retail rents in the world, at $2,000 per square foot ($22,000/m2) in 2023.[[351]](#cite_note-357)

New York City has one of the highest [costs of living](/wiki/Cost_of_living "Cost of living") in the world, which is exacerbated by the city's housing shortage.[[352]](#cite_note-358)[[353]](#cite_note-359) In 2023, one-bedroom apartments in Manhattan rented at a median monthly price of US$4,443.[[354]](#cite_note-360) The median house price city-wide is over $1 million as of 2023.[[355]](#cite_note-361) With 33,000 units available in 2023 among the city's 2.3 million rentable apartments, the vacancy rate was 1.4%, the lowest level since 1968 and a rate that is indicative of a shortage of available units, especially among those with rents below a monthly rental of $1,650, where less than 1% of units were available.[[356]](#cite_note-362) Perennially high demand has pushed median monthly one-bedroom apartment rents in New York City to over US$4,000 and two-bedroom rents to over $5,000, the highest in the United States by a significant margin.[[39]](#cite_note-NYCMostExpensiveRents-42)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in New York City](/wiki/Tourism_in_New_York_City "Tourism in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Times_Square%2C_Broadway%2C_May_2016.jpg/250px-Times_Square%2C_Broadway%2C_May_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Times_Square,_Broadway,_May_2016.jpg)

[Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square") is one of the world's leading tourist attractions with 50 million tourists annually.[[225]](#cite_note-NYCvisitors-230)

Tourism is a vital industry for New York City, and [New York City Tourism + Conventions](/wiki/New_York_City_Tourism_%2B_Conventions "New York City Tourism + Conventions") represents the city's official bureau of tourism.[[357]](#cite_note-363) New York has witnessed a growing combined volume of international and domestic tourists, with as many as 66.6 million visitors to the city per year, including as many as 13.5 million international visitors, with the highest numbers from the United Kingdom, Canada, Brazil, and China.[[358]](#cite_note-Tourism2021-364) Multiple sources have called New York the most photographed city in the world.[[359]](#cite_note-NYCMostPhotographed1-365)[[360]](#cite_note-NYCMostPhotographed2-366)[[361]](#cite_note-NYCMostPhotographed3-367) *[I Love New York](/wiki/I_Love_New_York "I Love New York")* (stylized I ❤ NY) is both a logo and a song that are the basis of an [advertising campaign](/wiki/Advertising_campaign "Advertising campaign") and have been used since 1977 to promote [tourism in New York City](/wiki/Tourism_in_New_York_City "Tourism in New York City"),[[362]](#cite_note-368) and later to promote New York State as well. The trademarked logo is owned by [New York State Empire State Development](/wiki/Empire_State_Development_Corporation "Empire State Development Corporation").[[363]](#cite_note-369)

Many [districts and monuments](/wiki/Lists_of_New_York_City_landmarks "Lists of New York City landmarks") in New York City are major landmarks, including three of the world's ten most-visited tourist attractions in 2023.[[364]](#cite_note-370) A record 66.6 million tourists visited New York City in 2019, bringing in $47.4 billion in tourism revenue. Visitor numbers dropped by two-thirds in 2020 during the pandemic, rebounding to 63.3 million in 2023.[[358]](#cite_note-Tourism2021-364)[[365]](#cite_note-371) Major landmarks in New York City include the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"), the [Statue of Liberty](/wiki/Statue_of_Liberty "Statue of Liberty"), the [Empire State Building](/wiki/Empire_State_Building "Empire State Building"), and Central Park.[[366]](#cite_note-372) Times Square is the brightly illuminated hub of the [Broadway Theater District](/wiki/Theater_District,_Manhattan "Theater District, Manhattan"),[[367]](#cite_note-373) and a major center of the world's [entertainment industry](/wiki/Entertainment_industry "Entertainment industry"),[[368]](#cite_note-374) attracting 50 million visitors annually to one of the world's busiest pedestrian intersections.[[225]](#cite_note-NYCvisitors-230) According to [The Broadway League](/wiki/The_Broadway_League "The Broadway League"), shows on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") sold approximately US$1.54 billion worth of tickets in both the 2022–2023 and the 2023–2024 seasons. Both seasons featured theater attendance of approximately 12.3 million each.[[369]](#cite_note-BroadwayLeagueStatistics-375)

### Media and entertainment

Main article: [Media in New York City](/wiki/Media_in_New_York_City "Media in New York City")

Further information: [New Yorkers in journalism](/wiki/New_Yorkers_in_journalism "New Yorkers in journalism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ce/New_York%2C_2019_%2848621790492%29.jpg/250px-New_York%2C_2019_%2848621790492%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York,_2019_(48621790492).jpg)

[Rockefeller Center](/wiki/Rockefeller_Center "Rockefeller Center"), one of Manhattan's leading [media and entertainment hubs](/wiki/Media_in_New_York_City "Media in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/94/Freedom_Of_Speech_%28256854761%29.jpeg/250px-Freedom_Of_Speech_%28256854761%29.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Freedom_Of_Speech_(256854761).jpeg)

[The headquarters](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Building "The New York Times Building") of [the New York Times Company](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Company "The New York Times Company"), publisher of *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")*

New York City has been described as the [entertainment](/wiki/Show_business "Show business")[[20]](#cite_note-NYCTheCapitaloftheWorld-22)[[370]](#cite_note-NYCTheEntertainmentCapitaloftheWorld-376)[[371]](#cite_note-377) and [digital media](/wiki/Digital_media "Digital media") capital of the world.[[372]](#cite_note-378) It is a center for the advertising, [music](/wiki/Music_of_New_York_City "Music of New York City"), [newspaper](/wiki/List_of_New_York_City_newspapers_and_magazines "List of New York City newspapers and magazines"), digital media, and publishing industries, and is the largest media market in North America.[[373]](#cite_note-379) Many of the world's largest [media conglomerates](/wiki/Media_conglomerate "Media conglomerate") are based in the city, including [Warner Bros. Discovery](/wiki/Warner_Bros._Discovery "Warner Bros. Discovery"), the [Thomson Reuters Corporation](/wiki/Thomson_Reuters_Corporation "Thomson Reuters Corporation"), the [Associated Press](/wiki/Associated_Press "Associated Press"), [Bloomberg L.P.](/wiki/Bloomberg_L.P. "Bloomberg L.P."), the [News Corp](/wiki/News_Corp "News Corp"), [The New York Times Company](/wiki/The_New_York_Times_Company "The New York Times Company"), [NBCUniversal](/wiki/NBCUniversal "NBCUniversal"), the [Hearst Corporation](/wiki/Hearst_Corporation "Hearst Corporation"), [AOL](/wiki/AOL "AOL") and [Fox Corporation](/wiki/Fox_Corporation "Fox Corporation"). Seven of the world's top eight global [advertising agency](/wiki/Advertising_agency "Advertising agency") networks have their headquarters in New York.[[374]](#cite_note-380)

More than 200 newspapers and 350 consumer magazines have an office in the city,[[375]](#cite_note-NYC_Media-381) and the publishing industry employs about 11,500 people, with an economic impact of $9.2 billion.[[376]](#cite_note-382) The two national daily newspapers with the largest daily [circulations](/wiki/Circulation_(newspaper) "Circulation (newspaper)") in the United States are published in New York: *[The Wall Street Journal](/wiki/The_Wall_Street_Journal "The Wall Street Journal")* and *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* broadsheets.[[377]](#cite_note-383) With 132 awards through 2022, *The Times* has won the most [Pulitzer Prizes](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize "Pulitzer Prize") for journalism[[378]](#cite_note-384) and is considered the U.S. media's [newspaper of record](/wiki/Newspaper_of_record "Newspaper of record").[[379]](#cite_note-385) [Tabloid](/wiki/Tabloid_(newspaper_format) "Tabloid (newspaper format)") newspapers in the city include the *[New York Daily News](/wiki/New_York_Daily_News "New York Daily News")*, which was founded in 1919 by [Joseph Medill Patterson](/wiki/Joseph_Medill_Patterson "Joseph Medill Patterson"),[[380]](#cite_note-386) and the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")*, founded in 1801 by [Alexander Hamilton](/wiki/Alexander_Hamilton "Alexander Hamilton").[[381]](#cite_note-387)[[382]](#cite_note-388)

As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit), New York City was the second-largest center for filmmaking and television production in the United States, producing about 200 feature films annually. The industry employed more than 100,000 people in 2019, generating $12.2 billion in wages and a total economic impact of $64.1 billion.[[383]](#cite_note-389) By volume, New York is the world leader in [independent film](/wiki/Independent_film "Independent film") production—one-third of all American independent films are produced there.[[384]](#cite_note-390)[[375]](#cite_note-NYC_Media-381)

New York is a major center for [non-commercial educational](/wiki/Non-commercial_educational "Non-commercial educational") media. [NYC Media](/wiki/NYC_Media "NYC Media") is the official public radio, television, and online media network and broadcasting service of New York City,[[385]](#cite_note-391) and has produced several original [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award")-winning shows covering music and culture in city neighborhoods and city government. The oldest [public-access television](/wiki/Public-access_television "Public-access television") channel in the United States is the [Manhattan Neighborhood Network](/wiki/Manhattan_Neighborhood_Network "Manhattan Neighborhood Network"), founded in 1971.[[386]](#cite_note-392) [WNET](/wiki/WNET "WNET") is the city's major public television station and produces a third of national [Public Broadcasting Service](/wiki/Public_Broadcasting_Service "Public Broadcasting Service") (PBS) television programming.[[387]](#cite_note-393) [WNYC](/wiki/WNYC "WNYC"), a [public radio](/wiki/Public_radio "Public radio") station owned by the city until 1997,[[388]](#cite_note-394) has the largest public radio audience in the United States.[[389]](#cite_note-395)

## Education

Main article: [Education in New York City](/wiki/Education_in_New_York_City "Education in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Low_Memorial_Library_Columbia_University_College_Walk_Court_Yard_05.jpg/250px-Low_Memorial_Library_Columbia_University_College_Walk_Court_Yard_05.jpg)](/wiki/File:Low_Memorial_Library_Columbia_University_College_Walk_Court_Yard_05.jpg)

The [Low Memorial Library](/wiki/Low_Memorial_Library "Low Memorial Library") at [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University")

New York City has the largest educational system of any city.[[20]](#cite_note-NYCTheCapitaloftheWorld-22) The city's educational infrastructure spans primary education, secondary education, higher education, and research. The [New York City Public Schools](/wiki/New_York_City_Public_Schools "New York City Public Schools") system, managed by the [New York City Department of Education](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Education "New York City Department of Education"), is the largest public school system in the United States, serving about 1.1 million students in approximately 1,800 separate primary and secondary schools, including [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_schools "Charter schools"), as of 2017–2018.[[390]](#cite_note-396) There are approximately 900 additional privately run secular and religious schools.[[391]](#cite_note-397)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Facade_of_the_New_York_Public_Library_Main_Branch_2.jpg/250px-Facade_of_the_New_York_Public_Library_Main_Branch_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Facade_of_the_New_York_Public_Library_Main_Branch_2.jpg)

The [Stephen A. Schwarzman Headquarters Building](/wiki/New_York_Public_Library_Main_Branch "New York Public Library Main Branch") of the [New York Public Library](/wiki/New_York_Public_Library "New York Public Library")

The [New York Public Library](/wiki/New_York_Public_Library "New York Public Library") (NYPL) has the largest collection of any public library system in the United States.[[392]](#cite_note-libraryspot-398) Queens is served by the [Queens Borough Public Library](/wiki/Queens_Borough_Public_Library "Queens Borough Public Library") (QPL), the nation's second-largest public library system, while the [Brooklyn Public Library](/wiki/Brooklyn_Public_Library "Brooklyn Public Library") (BPL) serves Brooklyn.[[392]](#cite_note-libraryspot-398)

More than a million students, the highest number of any city in the United States,[[393]](#cite_note-399) are enrolled in New York City's more than 120 higher education institutions, with nearly a quarter million in the [City University of New York](/wiki/City_University_of_New_York "City University of New York") (CUNY) system alone as of 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit).[[394]](#cite_note-400) According to [Academic Ranking of World Universities](/wiki/Academic_Ranking_of_World_Universities "Academic Ranking of World Universities"), New York City has, on average, the best higher education institutions of any [global city](/wiki/Global_city "Global city").[[395]](#cite_note-401)

The public CUNY system comprises 25 institutions across all five boroughs.[[396]](#cite_note-402) The public [State University of New York](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York "State University of New York") (SUNY) system's campuses in New York City include [SUNY Downstate Health Sciences University](/wiki/SUNY_Downstate_Health_Sciences_University "SUNY Downstate Health Sciences University"), [Fashion Institute of Technology](/wiki/Fashion_Institute_of_Technology "Fashion Institute of Technology"), [SUNY Maritime College](/wiki/SUNY_Maritime_College "SUNY Maritime College"), and [SUNY College of Optometry](/wiki/SUNY_College_of_Optometry "SUNY College of Optometry").[[397]](#cite_note-403) New York City is home to such notable private universities as [Adelphi University](/wiki/Adelphi_University "Adelphi University"), [Bank Street College of Education](/wiki/Bank_Street_College_of_Education "Bank Street College of Education"), [Barnard College](/wiki/Barnard_College "Barnard College"), [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), [Cooper Union](/wiki/Cooper_Union "Cooper Union"), [Fordham University](/wiki/Fordham_University "Fordham University"), [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University"), [New York Institute of Technology](/wiki/New_York_Institute_of_Technology "New York Institute of Technology"), [Rockefeller University](/wiki/Rockefeller_University "Rockefeller University"), [Mercy University](/wiki/Mercy_University "Mercy University"), [Cornell Tech](/wiki/Cornell_Tech "Cornell Tech") and [Yeshiva University](/wiki/Yeshiva_University "Yeshiva University"); several of these are ranked among the top universities in the world,[[398]](#cite_note-ARWU-404)[[399]](#cite_note-CWUR-405) while some of the world's most prestigious institutions, such as [Princeton University](/wiki/Princeton_University "Princeton University") and [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), are in the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area").

Much of the [scientific research](/wiki/Scientific_research "Scientific research") in the city is done in medicine and the [life sciences](/wiki/Life_sciences "Life sciences"). In 2019, the New York metropolitan area ranked first [by share of published articles in life sciences](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_scientific_output#Leading_cities_in_different_fields "List of cities by scientific output").[[400]](#cite_note-NYCLifeSciencesCapital-406) New York City has the most postgraduate life sciences degrees awarded annually in the United States, and in 2012, 43,523 licensed physicians were practicing in New York City.[[401]](#cite_note-407) There are 127 [Nobel laureates](/wiki/Nobel_laureate "Nobel laureate") with roots in local institutions as of 2004[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit).[[402]](#cite_note-NYC_science_institutions-408)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of New York City](/wiki/Culture_of_New_York_City "Culture of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/NYC_-_Guggenheim_Museum.jpg/250px-NYC_-_Guggenheim_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_-_Guggenheim_Museum.jpg)

[Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum](/wiki/Solomon_R._Guggenheim_Museum "Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum") seen from Fifth Avenue

New York City is frequently the [setting for novels, movies, and television programs](/wiki/List_of_films_set_in_New_York_City "List of films set in New York City") and has been described as the cultural capital of the world.[[403]](#cite_note-cultura1-409)[[404]](#cite_note-culture2-410)[[405]](#cite_note-culture3-411)[[406]](#cite_note-cultural4-412)
The city is the birthplace of many cultural movements, including the [Harlem Renaissance](/wiki/Harlem_Renaissance "Harlem Renaissance") in literature and visual art;[[407]](#cite_note-413)[[408]](#cite_note-414) [abstract expressionism](/wiki/Abstract_expressionism "Abstract expressionism") (known as the [New York School](/wiki/New_York_School_(art) "New York School (art)")) in painting; and [hip-hop](/wiki/Hip_hop_music "Hip hop music"),[[195]](#cite_note-Toop-1992-198)[[409]](#cite_note-BPOHPINY-415) [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"),[[410]](#cite_note-416) [hardcore](/wiki/Hardcore_punk "Hardcore punk"),[[411]](#cite_note-417) [salsa](/wiki/Salsa_music "Salsa music"), [freestyle](/wiki/Freestyle_music "Freestyle music"), [Tin Pan Alley](/wiki/Tin_Pan_Alley "Tin Pan Alley"), certain forms of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz"),[[412]](#cite_note-Jazzzz-418) and (along with Philadelphia) [disco](/wiki/Disco "Disco") in music. New York City has been considered the dance capital of the world.[[413]](#cite_note-419)[[414]](#cite_note-420)

One of the most common traits attributed to New York City is its fast pace,[[415]](#cite_note-FastPaceNYC1-421)[[416]](#cite_note-FastPaceNYC2-422)[[417]](#cite_note-FastPaceNYC3-423) which spawned the term *[New York minute](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/New_York_minute "wiktionary:New York minute")*.[[418]](#cite_note-NewYorkMinuteDefinition-424) New York City's residents are prominently known for their resilience historically, and more recently related to their management of the impacts of the [September 11 terrorist attacks](/wiki/September_11_terrorist_attacks "September 11 terrorist attacks") and the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_New_York_City "COVID-19 pandemic in New York City").[[419]](#cite_note-425)[[420]](#cite_note-Weaver-2022-426)[[421]](#cite_note-427) New York was voted the world's most resilient city in 2021 and 2022, per [*Time Out*](/wiki/Time_Out_(magazine) "Time Out (magazine)")'s global poll of urban residents.[[420]](#cite_note-Weaver-2022-426)

### Theater

Further information: [Broadway theatre](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") and [Theater District, Manhattan](/wiki/Theater_District,_Manhattan "Theater District, Manhattan")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Various_Pictures_from_New_York_City_-_September%2C_2025.jpg/250px-Various_Pictures_from_New_York_City_-_September%2C_2025.jpg)](/wiki/File:Various_Pictures_from_New_York_City_-_September,_2025.jpg)

The [Golden](/wiki/John_Golden_Theatre "John Golden Theatre"); [Jacobs](/wiki/Bernard_B._Jacobs_Theatre "Bernard B. Jacobs Theatre"); [Schoenfeld](/wiki/Gerald_Schoenfeld_Theatre "Gerald Schoenfeld Theatre"); and [Booth](/wiki/Booth_Theatre "Booth Theatre") theaters in the [Theater District](/wiki/Theater_District,_Manhattan "Theater District, Manhattan")

The central [hub of the American theater scene is Manhattan](/wiki/Theater_District,_Manhattan "Theater District, Manhattan"), with its divisions of [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre"), [off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-Broadway "Off-Broadway"), and [off-off-Broadway](/wiki/Off-off-Broadway "Off-off-Broadway").[[422]](#cite_note-LondréWatermeier1998-428) Many stage and screen [stars](/wiki/Celebrity "Celebrity") have gotten their big break working in New York productions.[[423]](#cite_note-429)

Broadway theatre is one of the premier forms of English-language theatre in the world, named after [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)"), the major thoroughfare that crosses [Times Square](/wiki/Times_Square "Times Square"),[[424]](#cite_note-430) sometimes referred to as "[The Great White Way](/wiki/The_Great_White_Way "The Great White Way")".[[425]](#cite_note-431)[[426]](#cite_note-432)[[427]](#cite_note-433)

[Forty-one theaters](/wiki/List_of_Broadway_theaters "List of Broadway theaters"), mostly in Midtown Manhattan's [Theater District](/wiki/Theater_District,_Manhattan "Theater District, Manhattan"), each with at least 500 seats, are classified as Broadway theaters.[[428]](#cite_note-434) The 2018–19 Broadway theatre season set records with total attendance of 14.8 million and gross revenue of $1.83 billion[[429]](#cite_note-435) Recovering from [closures forced by the COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/Impact_of_the_COVID-19_pandemic_on_the_performing_arts#Live_music_and_theatre "Impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on the performing arts"), 2022–23 revenues rebounded to $1.58 billion with total attendance of 12.3 million.[[430]](#cite_note-436)[[369]](#cite_note-BroadwayLeagueStatistics-375) The [Tony Awards](/wiki/Tony_Awards "Tony Awards") recognize excellence in live Broadway theatre and are presented annually by the [American Theatre Wing](/wiki/American_Theatre_Wing "American Theatre Wing") and [The Broadway League](/wiki/The_Broadway_League "The Broadway League")[[431]](#cite_note-437) in Manhattan.[[432]](#cite_note-438)

### Accent and dialect

Main articles: [New York City English](/wiki/New_York_City_English "New York City English") and [New York accent](/wiki/New_York_accent "New York accent")

The New York area is home to a distinctive regional accent and speech pattern called the [New York dialect](/wiki/New_York_City_English "New York City English"), alternatively known as *Brooklynese* or *New Yorkese*. It has been considered one of the most recognizable accents within [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English").[[433]](#cite_note-439) The traditional New York area speech pattern is known for its rapid delivery, and its accent is characterized as [non-rhotic](/wiki/Rhoticity_in_English "Rhoticity in English") so that the sound [ɹ] does not appear at the end of a [syllable](/wiki/Syllable "Syllable") or immediately before a [consonant](/wiki/Consonant "Consonant"), therefore the pronunciation of the city name as "New Yawk".[[434]](#cite_note-NYT19930214-440) The classic version of the New York City dialect is centered on [middle-](/wiki/Middle_class "Middle class") and working-class New Yorkers. The influx of non-European immigrants in recent decades has led to changes in this distinctive dialect,[[434]](#cite_note-NYT19930214-440) and the traditional form of this speech pattern is no longer as prevalent.[[434]](#cite_note-NYT19930214-440)

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of New York City](/wiki/Architecture_of_New_York_City "Architecture of New York City")

Further information: [List of buildings, sites, and monuments in New York City](/wiki/List_of_buildings,_sites,_and_monuments_in_New_York_City "List of buildings, sites, and monuments in New York City") and [List of tallest buildings in New York City](/wiki/List_of_tallest_buildings_in_New_York_City "List of tallest buildings in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/1232-42_Dean_Street_Crown_Heights.jpg/250px-1232-42_Dean_Street_Crown_Heights.jpg)](/wiki/File:1232-42_Dean_Street_Crown_Heights.jpg)

[Row houses](/wiki/Row_houses "Row houses") in [Crown Heights North Historic District](/wiki/Crown_Heights_North_Historic_District "Crown Heights North Historic District"), Brooklyn

New York has architecturally noteworthy buildings in a wide range of styles and from distinct periods, from the Dutch Colonial [Pieter Claesen Wyckoff House](/wiki/Wyckoff_House "Wyckoff House") in Brooklyn, the oldest section of which dates to 1656, to the modern [One World Trade Center](/wiki/One_World_Trade_Center "One World Trade Center"), the skyscraper at [Ground Zero](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_site "World Trade Center site") in Lower Manhattan and the [most expensive office tower in the world](/wiki/List_of_most_expensive_buildings_in_the_world "List of most expensive buildings in the world") by construction cost.[[435]](#cite_note-441)

Manhattan's [skyline](/wiki/Skyline "Skyline"), with its many skyscrapers, has been recognized as an iconic symbol of the city,[[436]](#cite_note-442)[[437]](#cite_note-443)[[438]](#cite_note-444) and the city has been home to several of the [tallest buildings in the world](/wiki/Skyscraper#History_of_the_tallest_skyscrapers "Skyscraper"). As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit), New York City had 6,455 high-rise buildings, the third most in the world after Hong Kong and [Seoul](/wiki/Seoul "Seoul").[[439]](#cite_note-445)

The character of New York's large residential districts is often defined by the elegant [brownstone](/wiki/Brownstone "Brownstone") rowhouses and [townhouses](/wiki/Townhouse "Townhouse") and shabby [tenements](/wiki/Tenement "Tenement") that were built during a period of rapid expansion from 1870 to 1930.[[440]](#cite_note-446) Stone and brick became the city's building materials of choice after the construction of wood-frame houses was limited in the aftermath of the [Great Fire of 1835](/wiki/Great_Fire_of_New_York "Great Fire of New York").[[441]](#cite_note-lankevich-p82-447)

In contrast, New York City also has neighborhoods that are less densely populated and feature free-standing dwellings. In neighborhoods such as [Riverdale](/wiki/Riverdale,_Bronx "Riverdale, Bronx") (in the Bronx), [Ditmas Park](/wiki/Ditmas_Park,_Brooklyn "Ditmas Park, Brooklyn") (in Brooklyn), and [Douglaston](/wiki/Douglaston,_Queens "Douglaston, Queens") (in Queens), large single-family homes are common in various architectural styles such as [Tudor Revival](/wiki/Tudor_Revival_architecture "Tudor Revival architecture") and [Victorian](/wiki/Victorian_architecture "Victorian architecture").[[442]](#cite_note-448)[[443]](#cite_note-449)[[444]](#cite_note-450)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/Midtown_Manhattan_from_Weehawken_September_2021_HDR_panorama.jpg/1280px-Midtown_Manhattan_from_Weehawken_September_2021_HDR_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Midtown_Manhattan_from_Weehawken_September_2021_HDR_panorama.jpg "High-resolution panorama of Midtown Manhattan, taken from Weehawken, New Jersey, on September 2021")

High-resolution panorama of [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"), taken from [Weehawken, New Jersey](/wiki/Weehawken,_New_Jersey "Weehawken, New Jersey"), on September 2021

### Arts

Further information: [List of museums and cultural institutions in New York City](/wiki/List_of_museums_and_cultural_institutions_in_New_York_City "List of museums and cultural institutions in New York City") and [Music of New York City](/wiki/Music_of_New_York_City "Music of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Lincoln_Center_at_dawn.jpg/250px-Lincoln_Center_at_dawn.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lincoln_Center_at_dawn.jpg)

The [Lincoln Center](/wiki/Lincoln_Center "Lincoln Center"): [David H. Koch Theater](/wiki/David_H._Koch_Theater "David H. Koch Theater") (left), home of the [NY City Ballet](/wiki/New_York_City_Ballet "New York City Ballet"); [Metropolitan Opera House](/wiki/Metropolitan_Opera_House_(Lincoln_Center) "Metropolitan Opera House (Lincoln Center)") (center), home of the [Metropolitan Opera](/wiki/Metropolitan_Opera "Metropolitan Opera"); and [David Geffen Hall](/wiki/David_Geffen_Hall "David Geffen Hall") (right), home of the [NY Philharmonic](/wiki/New_York_Philharmonic "New York Philharmonic")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_%28The_Met%29_-_Central_Park%2C_NYC.jpg/250px-Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_%28The_Met%29_-_Central_Park%2C_NYC.jpg)](/wiki/File:Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art_(The_Met)_-_Central_Park,_NYC.jpg)

[Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"), the largest [art museum](/wiki/Art_museum "Art museum") in the Americas

[Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts](/wiki/Lincoln_Center_for_the_Performing_Arts "Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts"), anchoring [Lincoln Square](/wiki/Lincoln_Square,_Manhattan "Lincoln Square, Manhattan") on the Upper West Side of Manhattan, is home to numerous influential arts organizations, including the [Metropolitan Opera](/wiki/Metropolitan_Opera "Metropolitan Opera"), [New York City Opera](/wiki/New_York_City_Opera "New York City Opera"), [New York Philharmonic](/wiki/New_York_Philharmonic "New York Philharmonic"), and [New York City Ballet](/wiki/New_York_City_Ballet "New York City Ballet"), as well as the [Vivian Beaumont Theater](/wiki/Vivian_Beaumont_Theater "Vivian Beaumont Theater"), the [Juilliard School](/wiki/Juilliard_School "Juilliard School"), [Jazz at Lincoln Center](/wiki/Jazz_at_Lincoln_Center "Jazz at Lincoln Center"), and [Alice Tully Hall](/wiki/Alice_Tully_Hall "Alice Tully Hall").
