The [Lee Strasberg Theatre and Film Institute](/wiki/Lee_Strasberg_Theatre_and_Film_Institute "Lee Strasberg Theatre and Film Institute") is in [Union Square](/wiki/Union_Square_(New_York_City) "Union Square (New York City)"), and [Tisch School of the Arts](/wiki/Tisch_School_of_the_Arts "Tisch School of the Arts") is based at New York University, while [Central Park SummerStage](/wiki/Central_Park_SummerStage "Central Park SummerStage") presents free concerts in Central Park.[[445]](#cite_note-451)

New York City has more than 2,000 arts and cultural organizations and more than 500 [art galleries](/wiki/Art_gallery "Art gallery").[[446]](#cite_note-NYC_arts-452) The city government funds the arts with a larger annual budget than the [National Endowment for the Arts](/wiki/National_Endowment_for_the_Arts "National Endowment for the Arts").[[446]](#cite_note-NYC_arts-452) The city is also home to hundreds of cultural institutions and historic sites. [Museum Mile](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue "Fifth Avenue") is the name for a section of Fifth Avenue running from 82nd to 105th streets on the [Upper East Side](/wiki/Upper_East_Side "Upper East Side") of Manhattan,[[447]](#cite_note-festival-453) in the upper portion of [Carnegie Hill](/wiki/Carnegie_Hill "Carnegie Hill").[[448]](#cite_note-454)

Nine museums occupy this section of Fifth Avenue, including the [Guggenheim](/wiki/Solomon_R._Guggenheim_Museum "Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum"), [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art"), [Neue Galerie New York](/wiki/Neue_Galerie_New_York "Neue Galerie New York"), the [Jewish Museum](/wiki/Jewish_Museum_(Manhattan) "Jewish Museum (Manhattan)"), and [The Africa Center](/wiki/The_Africa_Center "The Africa Center"), making it one of the densest displays of [high culture](/wiki/High_culture "High culture") in the world.[[449]](#cite_note-mmilemus-455) In addition to other programming, the museums collaborate for the annual Museum Mile Festival, held each year in June, to promote the museums and increase visitation.[[450]](#cite_note-drv-456) Many of the world's most lucrative [art auctions](/wiki/Art_auction "Art auction") are held in New York City.[[451]](#cite_note-NYCArtAuction1-457)[[452]](#cite_note-NYCArtAuction2-458)

The Metropolitan Museum of Art is the [largest art museum](/wiki/List_of_largest_art_museums "List of largest art museums") in the Americas. In 2022, it welcomed 3.2 million visitors, ranking it the [third-most visited museum in the country](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_museums_in_the_United_States "List of most-visited museums in the United States"), and [eighth-most visited art museum in the world](/wiki/List_of_most-visited_art_museums "List of most-visited art museums").[[453]](#cite_note-459) Its permanent collection contains more than two million works across 17 curatorial departments,[[454]](#cite_note-Metropolitan_Museum_Launches_New_and_Expanded_Web_Site-460) and includes works of art from [classical antiquity](/wiki/Classical_antiquity "Classical antiquity") and [ancient Egypt](/wiki/Art_of_ancient_Egypt "Art of ancient Egypt"); paintings and sculptures from nearly all the [European masters](/wiki/Western_painting "Western painting"); and an extensive collection of [American](/wiki/Visual_art_of_the_United_States "Visual art of the United States") and [modern art](/wiki/Modern_art "Modern art"). The Met maintains extensive holdings of [African](/wiki/African_art "African art"), [Asian](/wiki/Asian_art "Asian art"), [Oceanian](/wiki/Oceanian_art "Oceanian art"), [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_art "Byzantine art"), and [Islamic art](/wiki/Islamic_art "Islamic art").[[455]](#cite_note-461)

### Cuisine

Main articles: [Cuisine of New York City](/wiki/Cuisine_of_New_York_City "Cuisine of New York City"), [List of restaurants in New York City](/wiki/List_of_restaurants_in_New_York_City "List of restaurants in New York City"), and [List of Michelin-starred restaurants in New York City](/wiki/List_of_Michelin-starred_restaurants_in_New_York_City "List of Michelin-starred restaurants in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Lox-and-bagel-02.jpg/250px-Lox-and-bagel-02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lox-and-bagel-02.jpg)

[New York–style bagel](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93style_bagel "New York–style bagel") with [lox](/wiki/Lox "Lox")

New York City's food culture includes an array of international cuisines influenced by the city's long immigrant history. [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and Eastern European immigrants, especially [Jewish](/wiki/Jewish_Americans "Jewish Americans") immigrants from those regions, brought [New York-style bagels](/wiki/New_York-style_bagel "New York-style bagel"), [cheesecake](/wiki/Cheesecake#North_America "Cheesecake"), [hot dogs](/wiki/Hot_dog "Hot dog"), [knishes](/wiki/Knish "Knish"), and [delicatessens](/wiki/Delicatessen "Delicatessen") (delis) to the city. [Italian](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora") immigrants brought [New York-style pizza](/wiki/New_York-style_pizza "New York-style pizza") and [Italian cuisine](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine") into the city, while Jewish immigrants and Irish immigrants brought [pastrami](/wiki/Pastrami "Pastrami")[[456]](#cite_note-462) and [corned beef](/wiki/Corned_beef "Corned beef"),[[457]](#cite_note-463) respectively. [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_restaurant "Chinese restaurant") and other Asian restaurants, sandwich joints, [trattorias](/wiki/Trattoria "Trattoria"), [diners](/wiki/Diner "Diner"), and [coffeehouses](/wiki/Coffeehouse "Coffeehouse") are ubiquitous throughout the city. Some 4,000 mobile food vendors licensed by the city, many immigrant-owned, have made Middle Eastern foods such as [falafel](/wiki/Falafel "Falafel") and [kebabs](/wiki/Kebab "Kebab")[[458]](#cite_note-464) examples of modern New York [street food](/wiki/Street_food "Street food"). The city is home to "nearly one thousand of the finest and most diverse [haute cuisine](/wiki/Haute_cuisine "Haute cuisine") restaurants in the world", according to [Michelin](/wiki/Michelin "Michelin").[[459]](#cite_note-465) The [New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Health_and_Mental_Hygiene "New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene") assigns letter grades to the city's restaurants based on inspection results.[[460]](#cite_note-466) As of 2019, there were 27,043 restaurants in the city, up from 24,865 in 2017.[[461]](#cite_note-467) The [Queens Night Market](/wiki/Queens_Night_Market "Queens Night Market") in [Flushing Meadows–Corona Park](/wiki/Flushing_Meadows%E2%80%93Corona_Park "Flushing Meadows–Corona Park") attracts more than ten thousand people nightly to sample food from more than 85 countries.[[294]](#cite_note-NYCWorld-300)

### Fashion

Further information: [New York Fashion Week](/wiki/New_York_Fashion_Week "New York Fashion Week") and [Met Gala](/wiki/Met_Gala "Met Gala")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Carolina_Herrera_AW14_12.jpg/250px-Carolina_Herrera_AW14_12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carolina_Herrera_AW14_12.jpg)

[Haute couture](/wiki/Haute_couture "Haute couture") fashion models walk the [runway](/wiki/Catwalk "Catwalk") during [NYFW](/wiki/New_York_Fashion_Week "New York Fashion Week").

New York City is a global [fashion capital](/wiki/Fashion_capital "Fashion capital"), and the [fashion industry](/wiki/Fashion "Fashion") employs 4.6% of the city's private workforce.[[462]](#cite_note-NYCFashionCapital-468) New York Fashion Week (NYFW) is a high-profile semiannual event featuring [models](/wiki/Fashion_model "Fashion model") displaying the latest wardrobes created by fashion designers worldwide in advance of these fashions proceeding to the marketplace.[[463]](#cite_note-NewYorkFashionWeekRetailTrendsetter-469)

NYFW sets the tone for the global fashion industry.[[464]](#cite_note-NewYorkFashionWeekGlobalAndEconomicImpact-470) New York's fashion district encompasses roughly 30 city blocks in [Midtown Manhattan](/wiki/Midtown_Manhattan "Midtown Manhattan"),[[465]](#cite_note-NYCFashionDistrict-471) clustered around a stretch of [Seventh Avenue](/wiki/Seventh_Avenue_(Manhattan) "Seventh Avenue (Manhattan)") nicknamed *[Fashion Avenue](/wiki/Seventh_Avenue_(Manhattan)#Notable_districts_and_buildings "Seventh Avenue (Manhattan)")*.[[466]](#cite_note-NYCFashionAvenue-472) New York's fashion calendar also includes Couture Fashion Week to showcase [haute couture](/wiki/Haute_couture "Haute couture") styles.[[467]](#cite_note-NYCHauteCouture-473) The Met Gala is often described as "Fashion's biggest night".[[468]](#cite_note-MetGalaFashion'sBiggestNight-474)

### Parades

Further information: [List of parades in New York City](/wiki/Category:Parades_in_New_York_City "Category:Parades in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Macy%27s_Thanksgiving_Day_Parade_2022_New_York_City_%2852523112564%29.jpg/250px-Macy%27s_Thanksgiving_Day_Parade_2022_New_York_City_%2852523112564%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Macy%27s_Thanksgiving_Day_Parade_2022_New_York_City_(52523112564).jpg)

The annual [Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade](/wiki/Macy%27s_Thanksgiving_Day_Parade "Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade"), the world's largest parade[[469]](#cite_note-NYCThanksgivingParadeLargestWorld-475)

New York City is well known for its street [parades](/wiki/Parade "Parade"), the majority in Manhattan. The primary orientation of the annual street parades is typically from north to south, marching along major avenues. The annual [Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade](/wiki/Macy%27s_Thanksgiving_Day_Parade "Macy's Thanksgiving Day Parade") is the world's largest parade,[[469]](#cite_note-NYCThanksgivingParadeLargestWorld-475) beginning alongside Central Park and proceeding southward to the flagship [Macy's Herald Square](/wiki/Macy%27s_Herald_Square "Macy's Herald Square") store;[[470]](#cite_note-476) the parade is viewed on telecasts worldwide and draws millions of spectators in person.[[469]](#cite_note-NYCThanksgivingParadeLargestWorld-475) Other notable parades include the annual [New York City St. Patrick's Day Parade](/wiki/New_York_City_St._Patrick%27s_Day_Parade "New York City St. Patrick's Day Parade") in March, the [NYC LGBT Pride March](/wiki/NYC_Pride_March "NYC Pride March") in June, the LGBT-inspired [Greenwich Village Halloween Parade](/wiki/Greenwich_Village_Halloween_Parade "Greenwich Village Halloween Parade") in October, and numerous parades commemorating the independence days of many nations. [Ticker-tape parades](/wiki/List_of_ticker-tape_parades_in_New_York_City "List of ticker-tape parades in New York City") celebrating championships won by sports teams as well as other accomplishments march northward along the [Canyon of Heroes](/wiki/Canyon_of_Heroes "Canyon of Heroes") on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_(Manhattan) "Broadway (Manhattan)") from [Bowling Green](/wiki/Bowling_Green_(New_York_City) "Bowling Green (New York City)") to [City Hall Park](/wiki/City_Hall_Park "City Hall Park") in Lower Manhattan.

### Sports

Main articles: [Sports in the New York metropolitan area](/wiki/Sports_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Sports in the New York metropolitan area") and [Traditional games of New York City](/wiki/Traditional_games_of_New_York_City "Traditional games of New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/Arthur_ashe_stadium_interior.jpg/250px-Arthur_ashe_stadium_interior.jpg)](/wiki/File:Arthur_ashe_stadium_interior.jpg)

The [US Open Tennis Championships](/wiki/US_Open_(tennis) "US Open (tennis)") in [Flushing Meadows–Corona Park](/wiki/Flushing_Meadows%E2%80%93Corona_Park "Flushing Meadows–Corona Park") in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Citi_Field_2011.JPG/250px-Citi_Field_2011.JPG)](/wiki/File:Citi_Field_2011.JPG)

[Citi Field](/wiki/Citi_Field "Citi Field"), also in Flushing Meadows-Corona Park, has been home to the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets") since 2009.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Yankee_Stadium_overhead_2010.jpg/250px-Yankee_Stadium_overhead_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yankee_Stadium_overhead_2010.jpg)

[Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium") in [The Bronx](/wiki/The_Bronx "The Bronx") is home to the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees") and [New York City FC](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Barclays_Center_1.jpg/250px-Barclays_Center_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barclays_Center_1.jpg)

[Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center"), home to the [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets") of the [NBA](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") and the [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") of the [WNBA](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4b/Madison_Square_Garden_%28MSG%29_-_Full_%2848124330357%29.jpg/250px-Madison_Square_Garden_%28MSG%29_-_Full_%2848124330357%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Madison_Square_Garden_(MSG)_-_Full_(48124330357).jpg)

[Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden"), home to the [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") of the NBA and [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers") of the [NHL](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League")

New York City is home to the headquarters of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League"),[[471]](#cite_note-477) [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball"),[[472]](#cite_note-478) the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"),[[473]](#cite_note-479) the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"),[[474]](#cite_note-480) and [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer").[[475]](#cite_note-481)

New York City hosted the [1984 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/1984_Summer_Paralympics "1984 Summer Paralympics")[[476]](#cite_note-482) and the [1998 Goodwill Games](/wiki/1998_Goodwill_Games "1998 Goodwill Games").[[477]](#cite_note-483) New York City's [bid](/wiki/New_York_City_bid_for_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "New York City bid for the 2012 Summer Olympics") to host the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") was one of five finalists, but lost out to London.[[478]](#cite_note-484)

The city has played host to more than 40 major professional teams in the five sports and their respective competing leagues. Four of the ten most expensive stadiums ever built worldwide ([MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium"), the new [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium"), [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden"), and [Citi Field](/wiki/Citi_Field "Citi Field")) are in the New York metropolitan area.[[479]](#cite_note-485)

The city is represented in the National Football League by the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants "New York Giants") and the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets"), although both teams play their home games at MetLife Stadium in nearby [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey"),[[480]](#cite_note-486) which hosted [Super Bowl XLVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVIII "Super Bowl XLVIII") in 2014.[[481]](#cite_note-487)

The city's two Major League Baseball teams are the [New York Mets](/wiki/New_York_Mets "New York Mets"), who play at 41,800-seat Citi Field in Queens, and the [New York Yankees](/wiki/New_York_Yankees "New York Yankees"), who play at 47,400-seat [Yankee Stadium](/wiki/Yankee_Stadium "Yankee Stadium") in the Bronx.[[482]](#cite_note-488) The [two rivals compete](/wiki/Mets%E2%80%93Yankees_rivalry "Mets–Yankees rivalry") in six games of [interleague play](/wiki/Interleague_play "Interleague play") every regular season, called the [Subway Series](/wiki/Subway_Series "Subway Series").[[483]](#cite_note-489) The Yankees have won an MLB-record 27 championships,[[484]](#cite_note-490) while the Mets have won the [World Series](/wiki/World_Series "World Series") twice.[[485]](#cite_note-491) The city was once home to the Brooklyn Dodgers (now the [Los Angeles Dodgers](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Dodgers "Los Angeles Dodgers")), who won the World Series once,[[486]](#cite_note-492) and the [New York Giants](/wiki/New_York_Giants_(NL) "New York Giants (NL)") (now the [San Francisco Giants](/wiki/San_Francisco_Giants "San Francisco Giants")), who won the World Series five times. Both teams moved to California in 1958.[[487]](#cite_note-493) There is one [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") team in the city, the Mets-affiliated [Brooklyn Cyclones](/wiki/Brooklyn_Cyclones "Brooklyn Cyclones"),[[488]](#cite_note-494) and the city gained a club in the independent [Atlantic League](/wiki/Atlantic_League_of_Professional_Baseball "Atlantic League of Professional Baseball") when the [Staten Island FerryHawks](/wiki/Staten_Island_FerryHawks "Staten Island FerryHawks") began play in 2022.[[489]](#cite_note-495)

The city's National Basketball Association teams are the [New York Knicks](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks"), who play at [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden"), and the [Brooklyn Nets](/wiki/Brooklyn_Nets "Brooklyn Nets"), who play at the [Barclays Center](/wiki/Barclays_Center "Barclays Center"). The [New York Liberty](/wiki/New_York_Liberty "New York Liberty") is the city's [Women's National Basketball Association](/wiki/Women%27s_National_Basketball_Association "Women's National Basketball Association") team. The first national college-level basketball championship, the [National Invitation Tournament](/wiki/National_Invitation_Tournament "National Invitation Tournament"), was held in New York in 1938 and remains in the city.[[490]](#cite_note-496)

The metropolitan area is home to three National Hockey League teams. The [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers"), one of the league's [Original Six](/wiki/Original_Six "Original Six"), play at Madison Square Garden in Manhattan. The [New York Islanders](/wiki/New_York_Islanders "New York Islanders"), traditionally representing [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), play in [UBS Arena](/wiki/UBS_Arena "UBS Arena") in [Elmont, New York](/wiki/Elmont,_New_York "Elmont, New York"), but played in Brooklyn's Barclays Center from 2015 to 2020. The [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils") play at [Prudential Center](/wiki/Prudential_Center "Prudential Center") in nearby [Newark, New Jersey](/wiki/Newark,_New_Jersey "Newark, New Jersey").

New York City is represented by [New York City FC](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC") of Major League Soccer, who play their home games at Yankee Stadium[[491]](#cite_note-497) and the [New York Red Bulls](/wiki/New_York_Red_Bulls "New York Red Bulls"), who play their home games at [Sports Illustrated Stadium](/wiki/Sports_Illustrated_Stadium "Sports Illustrated Stadium") in nearby [Harrison, New Jersey](/wiki/Harrison,_New_Jersey "Harrison, New Jersey").[[492]](#cite_note-498) [Gotham FC](/wiki/Gotham_FC "Gotham FC") in the [National Women's Soccer League](/wiki/National_Women%27s_Soccer_League "National Women's Soccer League") plays their home games in Sports Illustrated Stadium. [Brooklyn FC](/wiki/Brooklyn_FC_(USL) "Brooklyn FC (USL)") is a professional soccer club based in that borough, fielding a women's team in the first division [USL Super League](/wiki/USL_Super_League "USL Super League") starting in 2024 and a men's team in the second division [USL Championship](/wiki/USL_Championship "USL Championship") in 2025.[[493]](#cite_note-shared-499) New York was a host city for the [1994 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup"), with matches being played at [Giants Stadium](/wiki/Giants_Stadium "Giants Stadium") in neighboring [East Rutherford, New Jersey](/wiki/East_Rutherford,_New_Jersey "East Rutherford, New Jersey").[[494]](#cite_note-500) New York City will be one of eleven host cities for the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup"), with the [final](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_final "2026 FIFA World Cup final") set to be played at MetLife Stadium.[[495]](#cite_note-501)[[496]](#cite_note-502)

The annual [US Open](/wiki/US_Open_(tennis) "US Open (tennis)") is one of four [Grand Slam](/wiki/Grand_Slam_(tennis) "Grand Slam (tennis)") tennis tournaments and is held at the [National Tennis Center](/wiki/USTA_National_Tennis_Center "USTA National Tennis Center") in [Flushing Meadows–Corona Park](/wiki/Flushing_Meadows%E2%80%93Corona_Park "Flushing Meadows–Corona Park").[[497]](#cite_note-503) The [New York City Marathon](/wiki/New_York_City_Marathon "New York City Marathon"), which courses through all five boroughs, is the world's largest running marathon, with 51,402 finishers in 2023, who came from all 50 states and 148 nations.[[498]](#cite_note-504) The [Millrose Games](/wiki/Millrose_Games "Millrose Games") is an annual track and field meet held at the [Fort Washington Avenue Armory](/wiki/Fort_Washington_Avenue_Armory "Fort Washington Avenue Armory"), whose featured event is the [Wanamaker Mile](/wiki/Wanamaker_Mile "Wanamaker Mile").[[499]](#cite_note-505) Boxing is a prominent part of the city's sporting scene, with events like the [New York Golden Gloves](/wiki/New_York_Golden_Gloves "New York Golden Gloves") held at Madison Square Garden each year.[[500]](#cite_note-506)

## Human resources

### Health

Main articles: [Healthcare in New York City](/wiki/Healthcare_in_New_York_City "Healthcare in New York City"), [NYC Health + Hospitals](/wiki/NYC_Health_%2B_Hospitals "NYC Health + Hospitals"), and [New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Health_and_Mental_Hygiene "New York City Department of Health and Mental Hygiene")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/NewYorkPresbyterian-Cornell.jpg/250px-NewYorkPresbyterian-Cornell.jpg)](/wiki/File:NewYorkPresbyterian-Cornell.jpg)

[New York-Presbyterian Hospital](/wiki/New_York-Presbyterian_Hospital "New York-Presbyterian Hospital"), affiliated with [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") and [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"), is the largest hospital and largest private employer in New York City and one of the world's busiest hospitals.[[501]](#cite_note-507)

New York City is a center for healthcare and medical training, with employment of over 750,000 in the city's health care sector.[[309]](#cite_note-NYCHealthCareLifeSciences-315)[[502]](#cite_note-508) Private hospitals in New York City include the [Hospital for Special Surgery](/wiki/Hospital_for_Special_Surgery "Hospital for Special Surgery"), [Lenox Hill Hospital](/wiki/Lenox_Hill_Hospital "Lenox Hill Hospital"), [Long Island Jewish Medical Center](/wiki/Long_Island_Jewish_Medical_Center "Long Island Jewish Medical Center"), [Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center](/wiki/Memorial_Sloan_Kettering_Cancer_Center "Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center"), [Mount Sinai Hospital](/wiki/Mount_Sinai_Hospital_(Manhattan) "Mount Sinai Hospital (Manhattan)"), [NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital](/wiki/NewYork-Presbyterian_Hospital "NewYork-Presbyterian Hospital"), and [NYU Langone Health](/wiki/NYU_Langone_Health "NYU Langone Health").[[503]](#cite_note-509) Medical schools include [SUNY Downstate College of Medicine](/wiki/SUNY_Downstate_College_of_Medicine "SUNY Downstate College of Medicine") in Brooklyn, [Albert Einstein College of Medicine](/wiki/Albert_Einstein_College_of_Medicine "Albert Einstein College of Medicine") in the Bronx, and [CUNY School of Medicine](/wiki/CUNY_School_of_Medicine "CUNY School of Medicine"), [Touro College of Osteopathic Medicine](/wiki/Touro_College_of_Osteopathic_Medicine "Touro College of Osteopathic Medicine"), [Columbia University Vagelos College of Physicians and Surgeons](/wiki/Columbia_University_Vagelos_College_of_Physicians_and_Surgeons "Columbia University Vagelos College of Physicians and Surgeons"), [Weill Cornell Medicine](/wiki/Weill_Cornell_Medicine "Weill Cornell Medicine"), [Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai](/wiki/Icahn_School_of_Medicine_at_Mount_Sinai "Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai"), and [New York University School of Medicine](/wiki/New_York_University_School_of_Medicine "New York University School of Medicine") in Manhattan.[[504]](#cite_note-510)

[NYC Health + Hospitals](/wiki/NYC_Health_%2B_Hospitals "NYC Health + Hospitals") (HHC) is a [public-benefit corporation](/wiki/New_York_state_public-benefit_corporations "New York state public-benefit corporations") established in 1969 which operates the city's [public hospitals](/wiki/Public_hospital#United_States "Public hospital") and a network of [outpatient](/wiki/Outpatient_care "Outpatient care") clinics.[[505]](#cite_note-511)[[506]](#cite_note-512) As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit), HHC is the largest American municipal healthcare system with $10.9 billion in annual revenues.[[507]](#cite_note-513) HHC serves 1.4 million patients, including more than 475,000 uninsured city residents.[[508]](#cite_note-HHC1-514) HHC operates eleven [acute-care](/wiki/Acute_care "Acute care") hospitals, four [skilled nursing facilities](/wiki/Skilled_nursing_facilities "Skilled nursing facilities"), six diagnostic and treatment centers, and more than 70 community-based [primary care](/wiki/Primary_care "Primary care") sites, serving primarily the city's poor and working-class residents.[[509]](#cite_note-515)[[510]](#cite_note-516) HHC's MetroPlus Health Plan is one of New York City's largest providers of government-sponsored health insurance, enrolling 670,000 city residents as of June 2022.[[511]](#cite_note-517)

HHC's facilities annually provides service to millions of New Yorkers, interpreted in more than 190 languages.[[512]](#cite_note-HHC2-518) The best-known hospital in the HHC system is [Bellevue Hospital](/wiki/Bellevue_Hospital "Bellevue Hospital"), the oldest public hospital in the United States, established in 1736.[[513]](#cite_note-519) Bellevue is the designated hospital for treatment of the president and other [world leaders](/wiki/List_of_current_heads_of_state_and_government "List of current heads of state and government") should they require care while in New York City.[[514]](#cite_note-Funding_Universe_Web_Site-520)

The city banned smoking in most parts of restaurants in 1995 and prohibited smoking in bars, restaurants, and places of public employment in 2003.[[515]](#cite_note-521) [Pharmacies](/wiki/Pharmacies "Pharmacies") are banned from selling smoked and vaped products in New York State.[[516]](#cite_note-NoTobaccoNYPharmacies-522)

New York City enforces a [right-to-shelter](/wiki/Right_to_housing "Right to housing") law guaranteeing shelter to anyone who needs it, regardless of their immigration, socioeconomic, or housing status, which entails providing adequate shelter and food.[[517]](#cite_note-NYCRightToShelter-523) As a result, while New York has the highest total homeless population of American cities, in 2024 only 5% were unsheltered by the city, representing a significantly lower percentage of outdoor homelessness than in other cities.[[518]](#cite_note-MostNYCHomelessAreSheltered-524) As of 2023, there were 92,824 [homeless people](/wiki/Homelessness_in_the_United_States "Homelessness in the United States") sleeping nightly in the shelter system.[[519]](#cite_note-525)

### Public safety

Main articles: [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department"), [New York City Fire Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Fire_Department "New York City Fire Department"), [Crime in New York City](/wiki/Crime_in_New_York_City "Crime in New York City"), and [Law enforcement in New York City](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_New_York_City "Law enforcement in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/My_picture_of_NYPD_officers.jpg/250px-My_picture_of_NYPD_officers.jpg)](/wiki/File:My_picture_of_NYPD_officers.jpg)

[New York Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") (NYPD) police officers in Brooklyn

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/FDNY_Tower_Ladder_1_%28897367891%29.jpg/250px-FDNY_Tower_Ladder_1_%28897367891%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:FDNY_Tower_Ladder_1_(897367891).jpg)

The [Fire Department of New York](/wiki/New_York_City_Fire_Department "New York City Fire Department") (FDNY), the largest municipal fire department in the United States

The [New York Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") (NYPD) is the largest police force in the United States, with more than 36,000 sworn officers.[[520]](#cite_note-526) Members of the NYPD are frequently referred to by politicians, the media, and their own police cars by the nickname, *New York's Finest*.[[521]](#cite_note-527)

The city saw a spike in crime in the 1970s through the 1990s.[[522]](#cite_note-Prager-528) Crime overall has trended downward in New York City since the 1990s;[[523]](#cite_note-529) violent crime decreased more than 75% from 1993 to 2005, and continued decreasing during periods when the nation as a whole saw increases.[[524]](#cite_note-Law-530) The [NYPD's stop-and-frisk](/wiki/Stop-and-frisk_in_New_York_City "Stop-and-frisk in New York City") program was declared unconstitutional in 2013 as a "policy of indirect [racial profiling](/wiki/Racial_profiling "Racial profiling")" of Black and Mixed residents,[[525]](#cite_note-531) although claims of disparate impact continued in subsequent years.[[526]](#cite_note-532) The stop-and-frisk program had been widely credited as being behind the decline in crime, though rates continued dropping in the years after the program ended.[[527]](#cite_note-533)[[528]](#cite_note-534)

The city set a record high of 2,245 murders in 1990 and hit a near-70-year record low of 289 in 2018.[[529]](#cite_note-535) The number of murders and the rate of 3.3 per 100,000 residents in 2017 was the lowest since 1951.[[530]](#cite_note-536) New York City recorded 386 murders in 2023, a decline of 12% from the previous year.[[531]](#cite_note-537)[[532]](#cite_note-538) New York City had [one of the lowest homicide rates among the ten largest U.S. cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_by_homicide_rate "List of cities by homicide rate") at 5.5 per 100,000 residents in 2021.[[533]](#cite_note-539)

New York City [has stricter gun laws](/wiki/Gun_Laws_in_New_York_City "Gun Laws in New York City") than most [other cities in the United States](/wiki/Gun_law_in_the_United_States "Gun law in the United States")—a license to own any firearm is required, and the [NY SAFE Act](/wiki/NY_SAFE_Act "NY SAFE Act") of 2013 [banned assault weapons](/wiki/Assault_weapons_legislation_in_the_United_States "Assault weapons legislation in the United States"). New York State had the fifth-lowest gun death rate of the states in 2020.[[534]](#cite_note-540)

[Organized crime](/wiki/Organized_crime "Organized crime") has long been associated with New York City, beginning with the [Forty Thieves](/wiki/Forty_Thieves_(New_York_gang) "Forty Thieves (New York gang)") and the [Roach Guards](/wiki/Roach_Guards "Roach Guards") in the [Five Points](/wiki/Five_Points,_Manhattan "Five Points, Manhattan") neighborhood in the 1820s, followed by the [Tongs](/wiki/Tong_(organization) "Tong (organization)") in the same neighborhood, which ultimately evolved into Chinatown, Manhattan. The 20th century saw a rise in the [Mafia](/wiki/American_Mafia "American Mafia"), dominated by the [Five Families](/wiki/Five_Families "Five Families"), as well as in [gangs](/wiki/Gang "Gang"), including the [Black Spades](/wiki/Black_Spades "Black Spades").[[535]](#cite_note-541) The Mafia and gang presence has declined in the city in the 21st century.[[536]](#cite_note-542)[[537]](#cite_note-543)

The [Fire Department of New York](/wiki/New_York_City_Fire_Department "New York City Fire Department") (FDNY) provides [fire protection](/wiki/Fire_protection "Fire protection"), technical rescue, primary response to biological, chemical, and radioactive hazards, and [emergency medical services](/wiki/Emergency_medical_services "Emergency medical services"). FDNY faces multifaceted firefighting challenges in many ways unique to New York. In addition to responding to [building types](/wiki/List_of_building_types "List of building types") that range from wood-frame single family homes to [high-rise structures](/wiki/High-rise "High-rise"), the FDNY responds to fires that occur in the [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway").[[538]](#cite_note-544) Secluded bridges and tunnels, as well as large parks and wooded areas that can give rise to brush fires, also present challenges. The FDNY is headquartered at [9 MetroTech Center](/wiki/9_MetroTech_Center "9 MetroTech Center") in [Downtown Brooklyn](/wiki/Downtown_Brooklyn "Downtown Brooklyn"),[[539]](#cite_note-545) and the FDNY Fire Academy is on [Randalls Island](/wiki/Randalls_and_Wards_Islands "Randalls and Wards Islands").[[540]](#cite_note-546)

## Transportation

Main article: [Transportation in New York City](/wiki/Transportation_in_New_York_City "Transportation in New York City")

### Rapid transit

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1c/Port-authority-terminal.jpg/250px-Port-authority-terminal.jpg)](/wiki/File:Port-authority-terminal.jpg)

[Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "Port Authority Bus Terminal"), the world's busiest bus station, at [Eighth Avenue](/wiki/Eighth_Avenue_(Manhattan) "Eighth Avenue (Manhattan)") and [42nd Street](/wiki/42nd_Street_(Manhattan) "42nd Street (Manhattan)")[[541]](#cite_note-Record2021-547)[[542]](#cite_note-PABT2008-548)

Mass transit in New York City, most of which runs 24 hours a day, accounts for one in every three users of mass transit in the country, and two-thirds of the nation's rail riders live in the New York City metropolitan area.[[543]](#cite_note-MTAinfo-549)[[544]](#cite_note-550)

#### Buses

New York City's public [bus fleet](/wiki/MTA_Regional_Bus_Operations "MTA Regional Bus Operations") runs [24/7](/wiki/24/7_service "24/7 service") and is the largest in North America.[[545]](#cite_note-551) The New York City bus system serves the most passengers of any city in the nation: In 2022, [MTA New York City Transit](/wiki/New_York_City_Transit_Authority "New York City Transit Authority")'s buses served 483.5 million trips, while [MTA Regional Bus Operations](/wiki/MTA_Regional_Bus_Operations "MTA Regional Bus Operations") handled 100.3 million trips.[[546]](#cite_note-552)

The [Port Authority Bus Terminal](/wiki/Port_Authority_Bus_Terminal "Port Authority Bus Terminal") is the city's main [intercity bus](/wiki/Intercity_bus "Intercity bus") terminal and the world's busiest bus station, serving 250,000 passengers on 7,000 buses each workday in a building opened in 1950 that was designed to accommodate 60,000 daily passengers. A 2021 plan announced by the Port Authority would spend $10 billion to expand capacity and modernize the facility.[[542]](#cite_note-PABT2008-548)[[547]](#cite_note-NYT2021-553)[[541]](#cite_note-Record2021-547) In 2024, the Port Authority announced plans for a new terminal that would feature a glass atrium at a new main entrance on 41st Street.[[548]](#cite_note-554)[[549]](#cite_note-555)

#### Rail

Main article: [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway")

[![A row of yellow taxis in front of a multi-story ornate stone building with three huge arched windows.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Image-Grand_central_Station_Outside_Night_2.jpg/250px-Image-Grand_central_Station_Outside_Night_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Image-Grand_central_Station_Outside_Night_2.jpg)

New York City is home to the two busiest train stations in the United States, [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") (pictured) and [Penn Station](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Station_(New_York_City) "Pennsylvania Station (New York City)").

[![The front end of a subway train, with a red E on a LED display on the top. To the right of the train is a platform with a group of people waiting for their train.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/R160_E_enters_42nd_Street.jpg/250px-R160_E_enters_42nd_Street.jpg)](/wiki/File:R160_E_enters_42nd_Street.jpg)

The [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway"), the world's largest [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system by number of [stations](/wiki/Train_station "Train station")

The [New York City Subway](/wiki/New_York_City_Subway "New York City Subway") system is the largest [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system in the world when measured by stations in operation, with 472, and by length of routes. Nearly all of New York's subway system is open 24 hours a day, in contrast to the overnight shutdown common to most subway systems.[[550]](#cite_note-556) The New York City Subway is [the busiest metropolitan rail transit system in the Western Hemisphere](/wiki/Metro_systems_by_annual_passenger_rides "Metro systems by annual passenger rides"),[[551]](#cite_note-Railway_Technology_2014_m729-557) with 1.70 billion passenger rides in 2019.[[552]](#cite_note-MTA_p768-558)

Public transport is widely used in New York City. 54.6% of New Yorkers commuted to work in 2005 using [mass transit](/wiki/Public_transport "Public transport").[[553]](#cite_note-559) This is in contrast to the rest of the country, where 91% of commuters travel in automobiles to their workplace.[[554]](#cite_note-2001summary-560) According to the [New York City Comptroller](/wiki/New_York_City_Comptroller "New York City Comptroller"), workers in the New York City area spend an average of 6 hours and 18 minutes getting to work each week, the longest commute time in the nation among large cities.[[555]](#cite_note-561) New York is the only American city in which a majority (52%) of households do not have a car; only 22% of Manhattanites own a car.[[556]](#cite_note-562) Due to their [high usage of mass transit](/wiki/List_of_U.S._cities_with_high_transit_ridership "List of U.S. cities with high transit ridership"), New Yorkers spend less of their household income on transportation than the national average, saving $19 billion annually on transportation compared to other urban Americans.[[557]](#cite_note-563)

New York City's [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") network is the largest in North America.[[543]](#cite_note-MTAinfo-549) The rail network, connecting New York City to its suburbs, consists of the [Long Island Rail Road](/wiki/Long_Island_Rail_Road "Long Island Rail Road"), [Metro-North Railroad](/wiki/Metro-North_Railroad "Metro-North Railroad"), and [New Jersey Transit](/wiki/New_Jersey_Transit_rail_operations "New Jersey Transit rail operations"). The combined systems converge at Grand Central Terminal and [New York Penn Station](/wiki/New_York_Penn_Station "New York Penn Station") and contain more than 250 stations and 20 rail lines.[[543]](#cite_note-MTAinfo-549) The elevated [AirTrain JFK](/wiki/AirTrain_JFK "AirTrain JFK") in Queens connects [JFK International Airport](/wiki/JFK_International_Airport "JFK International Airport") to the New York City Subway and the Long Island Rail Road.[[558]](#cite_note-564) For [inter-city rail](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail"), New York City is served by [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak"), whose busiest station by a significant margin is Penn Station on the [West Side](/wiki/West_Side_(Manhattan) "West Side (Manhattan)") of Manhattan, from which Amtrak provides connections to Boston, Philadelphia, and Washington, D.C. along the [Northeast Corridor](/wiki/Northeast_Corridor "Northeast Corridor"), and long-distance train service to other North American cities.[[559]](#cite_note-565)

The [Staten Island Railway](/wiki/Staten_Island_Railway "Staten Island Railway") rapid transit system solely serves Staten Island, operating 24 hours a day, with access to Manhattan from the [St. George Terminal](/wiki/St._George_Terminal "St. George Terminal") via the [Staten Island Ferry](/wiki/Staten_Island_Ferry "Staten Island Ferry").[[560]](#cite_note-566) The [PATH](/wiki/PATH_(rail_system) "PATH (rail system)") train links Midtown and Lower Manhattan with [Hoboken Terminal](/wiki/Hoboken_Terminal "Hoboken Terminal") and [Newark Penn Station](/wiki/Newark_Penn_Station "Newark Penn Station") in New Jersey, and then those stations with the [World Trade Center Oculus](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_station_(PATH) "World Trade Center station (PATH)") across the Hudson River.[[561]](#cite_note-567) Like the New York City Subway, the PATH operates 24 hours a day, meaning three of the five American rapid transit systems that operate on 24-hour schedules are wholly or partly in New York.[[562]](#cite_note-568) [Grand Central Terminal](/wiki/Grand_Central_Terminal "Grand Central Terminal") is the world's largest train station by number of [rail platforms](/wiki/Rail_platform "Rail platform") and acres occupied.[[563]](#cite_note-GrandCentralLargestTrainStationWorld-569)

Multibillion-dollar [heavy rail](/wiki/Heavy_rail "Heavy rail") transit projects under construction in New York City include the [Second Avenue Subway](/wiki/Second_Avenue_Subway "Second Avenue Subway").[[564]](#cite_note-570)

### Air

Main article: [Aviation in the New York metropolitan area](/wiki/Aviation_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Aviation in the New York metropolitan area")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/JFK_Aerial_Nov_14_2018.jpg/250px-JFK_Aerial_Nov_14_2018.jpg)](/wiki/File:JFK_Aerial_Nov_14_2018.jpg)

[John F. Kennedy Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") in [Queens](/wiki/Queens "Queens")

[New York's airspace](/wiki/Aviation_in_the_New_York_metropolitan_area "Aviation in the New York metropolitan area") is the busiest in the United States and one of the world's busiest air corridors. The three busiest airports in the New York metropolitan area are [John F. Kennedy International Airport](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy_International_Airport "John F. Kennedy International Airport") (with 55.3 million passengers), [Newark Liberty International Airport](/wiki/Newark_Liberty_International_Airport "Newark Liberty International Airport") (43.6 million) and [LaGuardia Airport](/wiki/LaGuardia_Airport "LaGuardia Airport") (29.0 million); 127.9 million travelers used these three airports in 2022.[[565]](#cite_note-571) JFK and Newark Liberty were the [busiest and fourth-busiest U.S. gateways](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_the_United_States#10_busiest_US_airports_by_international_passenger_traffic_(2012) "List of the busiest airports in the United States") for international air passengers, respectively, in 2023.[[566]](#cite_note-572) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit), JFK was the [busiest airport for international passengers](/wiki/List_of_busiest_airports_by_international_passenger_traffic "List of busiest airports by international passenger traffic") in North America, with 26.8 million international passengers.[[567]](#cite_note-573)

Described in 2014 by then–Vice President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") as the kind of airport travelers would see in "some third world country", LaGuardia Airport has undergone an $8 billion project with federal and state support that has replaced its aging facilities with modern terminals and roadways.[[568]](#cite_note-574)[[569]](#cite_note-575)[[570]](#cite_note-576)[[571]](#cite_note-577) Plans have advanced to expand passenger volume at a fourth airport, [Stewart International Airport](/wiki/Stewart_International_Airport "Stewart International Airport"), near [Newburgh, New York](/wiki/Newburgh,_New_York "Newburgh, New York"), by the [Port Authority of New York and New Jersey](/wiki/Port_Authority_of_New_York_and_New_Jersey "Port Authority of New York and New Jersey").[[572]](#cite_note-578) Other commercial airports in or serving the [New York metropolitan area](/wiki/New_York_metropolitan_area "New York metropolitan area") include [Long Island MacArthur Airport](/wiki/Long_Island_MacArthur_Airport "Long Island MacArthur Airport"), [Trenton–Mercer Airport](/wiki/Trenton%E2%80%93Mercer_Airport "Trenton–Mercer Airport") and [Westchester County Airport](/wiki/Westchester_County_Airport "Westchester County Airport"). The primary general aviation airport serving the area is [Teterboro Airport](/wiki/Teterboro_Airport "Teterboro Airport").

### Ferries, taxis and trams

Main articles: [Staten Island Ferry](/wiki/Staten_Island_Ferry "Staten Island Ferry"), [NYC Ferry](/wiki/NYC_Ferry "NYC Ferry"), [Taxis of New York City](/wiki/Taxis_of_New_York_City "Taxis of New York City"), and [Roosevelt Island Tramway](/wiki/Roosevelt_Island_Tramway "Roosevelt Island Tramway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Spirit_of_America_-_Staten_Island_Ferry.jpg/250px-Spirit_of_America_-_Staten_Island_Ferry.jpg)](/wiki/File:Spirit_of_America_-_Staten_Island_Ferry.jpg)

The Staten Island Ferry shuttles [commuters](/wiki/Commuting "Commuting") between [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and [Staten Island](/wiki/Staten_Island "Staten Island").

The Staten Island Ferry is the world's busiest ferry, carrying more than 23 million passengers from July 2015 through June 2016 on a 5.2-mile (8.4 km) route between Staten Island and Lower Manhattan and running 24/7.[[573]](#cite_note-579)[[574]](#cite_note-nycgov-official-580) Other ferry systems shuttle commuters between Manhattan and other locales within the city and the metropolitan area. NYC Ferry, a [NYCEDC](/wiki/New_York_City_Economic_Development_Corporation "New York City Economic Development Corporation") initiative with routes planned to travel to all five boroughs, was launched in 2017.[[575]](#cite_note-581)

Identified by their color and [taxi medallion](/wiki/Taxi_medallion "Taxi medallion"), the city's 13,587 yellow taxicabs are the only vehicles allowed to pick up riders making street hails throughout the city.[[576]](#cite_note-582) [Apple green](/wiki/Apple_green "Apple green")-colored [boro taxis](/wiki/Boro_taxi "Boro taxi") can pick up street hails in [Upper Manhattan](/wiki/Upper_Manhattan "Upper Manhattan") and the four outer boroughs.[[577]](#cite_note-583) Long dominated by yellow taxis, [high-volume for-hire vehicles](/wiki/Vehicle_for_hire "Vehicle for hire") from [Uber](/wiki/Uber "Uber") and [Lyft](/wiki/Lyft "Lyft") have provided the most trips in the city since December 2016, when the for-hire vehicles and cabs each had about 10.5 million trips. By October 2023, the 78,000 vehicles-for-hire combined for 20.3 million trips, while 3.5 million trips were in yellow taxis.[[578]](#cite_note-584)[[579]](#cite_note-585)

The Roosevelt Island Tramway, an [aerial tramway](/wiki/Aerial_tramway "Aerial tramway") that began operation in 1976,[[580]](#cite_note-586) transports 2 million passengers per year the 3,140 feet (960 m) between Roosevelt Island and [59th Street](/wiki/59th_Street_(Manhattan) "59th Street (Manhattan)") and [Second Avenue](/wiki/Second_Avenue_(Manhattan) "Second Avenue (Manhattan)") on Manhattan Island.[[581]](#cite_note-587)

### Cycling network

Main article: [Cycling in New York City](/wiki/Cycling_in_New_York_City "Cycling in New York City")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Citi_bike_%2810414610734%29.jpg/250px-Citi_bike_%2810414610734%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Citi_bike_(10414610734).jpg)

[Citi Bike](/wiki/Citi_Bike "Citi Bike") bike share service, which started in May 2013

New York City has mixed cycling conditions, which include urban density, relatively flat terrain, congested roadways with stop-and-go traffic, and many pedestrians. The city's large cycling population includes [utility cyclists](/wiki/Utility_cycling "Utility cycling"), such as delivery and messenger services; recreational [cycling clubs](/wiki/Cycling_club "Cycling club"); and an increasing number of [commuters](/wiki/Bicycle_commuting "Bicycle commuting"). Cycling is increasingly popular in New York City; in 2022 there were approximately 61,200 people who commuted daily using a bicycle and 610,000 daily bike trips, both nearly doubling over the previous decade.[[231]](#cite_note-NYCcycling-236) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=New_York_City&action=edit), New York City had 1,525 miles (2,454 km) of [bike lanes](/wiki/Bike_lane "Bike lane"), including 644 miles (1,036 km) of segregated or "protected" bike lanes citywide.[[231]](#cite_note-NYCcycling-236)

### Streets and highways

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Manhattanhenge_2016-07-12-FRD.png/250px-Manhattanhenge_2016-07-12-FRD.png)](/wiki/File:Manhattanhenge_2016-07-12-FRD.png)

Tourists observing [Manhattanhenge](/wiki/Manhattanhenge "Manhattanhenge") on [42nd Street](/wiki/42nd_Street_(Manhattan) "42nd Street (Manhattan)") on July 12, 2016

Streets are also a defining feature of the city. New York has been found to lead the world in urban automobile [traffic congestion](/wiki/Traffic_congestion "Traffic congestion").[[36]](#cite_note-NYCCongestion-39) The [Commissioners' Plan of 1811](/wiki/Commissioners%27_Plan_of_1811 "Commissioners' Plan of 1811") greatly influenced its physical development. New York City has an extensive web of freeways and [parkways](/wiki/Parkway "Parkway"), which link the city's boroughs to each other and [North Jersey](/wiki/North_Jersey "North Jersey"), Westchester County, Long Island, and southwestern [Connecticut](/wiki/Connecticut "Connecticut") through [bridges and tunnels](/wiki/Bridges_and_tunnels_in_New_York_City "Bridges and tunnels in New York City"). Because these highways serve millions of outer borough and suburban residents who [commute](/wiki/Commuting "Commuting") into Manhattan, it is common for motorists to be stranded for hours in dense traffic congestion that is a daily occurrence, particularly during [rush hour](/wiki/Rush_hour "Rush hour").[[582]](#cite_note-588)[[583]](#cite_note-589) [Congestion pricing in New York City](/wiki/Congestion_pricing_in_New_York_City "Congestion pricing in New York City"), first such program in the nation,[[584]](#cite_note-590) was activated in January 2025, applying to most motor vehicular traffic using the area of Manhattan south of [60th Street](/wiki/60th_Street_(Manhattan) "60th Street (Manhattan)"), in an effort to encourage commuters to use [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") instead.[[585]](#cite_note-ManhattanCongestionPricing-591)
Unlike the rest of the country, New York State prohibits [turns on red lights](/wiki/Turn_on_red "Turn on red") in cities with a population greater than one million, to reduce collisions and increase pedestrian safety. In New York City, therefore, all turns on red lights are illegal unless a sign permitting such maneuvers is present.[[586]](#cite_note-592)

### Bridges and tunnels

Further information: [List of bridges and tunnels in New York City](/wiki/List_of_bridges_and_tunnels_in_New_York_City "List of bridges and tunnels in New York City") and [Commissioners' Plan of 1811](/wiki/Commissioners%27_Plan_of_1811 "Commissioners' Plan of 1811")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Manhattan_and_Brooklyn_bridges_on_the_East_River%2C_New_York_City%2C_1981.jpg/250px-Manhattan_and_Brooklyn_bridges_on_the_East_River%2C_New_York_City%2C_1981.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manhattan_and_Brooklyn_bridges_on_the_East_River,_New_York_City,_1981.jpg)

The [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") and [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge") on the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River")

The boroughs of Manhattan and Staten Island are located on islands with the same names, while Queens and Brooklyn are at the western end of the larger Long Island, and the Bronx is on New York State's mainland. Manhattan Island is linked to the outer boroughs and New Jersey by an extensive network of bridges and tunnels. The 14-lane [George Washington Bridge](/wiki/George_Washington_Bridge "George Washington Bridge"), connecting Manhattan to New Jersey across the Hudson River, is the world's busiest motor vehicle bridge.[[587]](#cite_note-gwbridge-593)[[588]](#cite_note-gwbridgepainters-594) The [Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge](/wiki/Verrazzano%E2%80%93Narrows_Bridge "Verrazzano–Narrows Bridge"), spanning [the Narrows](/wiki/The_Narrows "The Narrows") between Brooklyn and Staten Island, is the longest [suspension bridge](/wiki/Suspension_bridge "Suspension bridge") in the Americas and one of the world's longest.[[589]](#cite_note-infoplease.com-595)[[590]](#cite_note-Verrazano-Narrows_Bridge-596) The [Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge "Brooklyn Bridge"), with its stone neo-Gothic suspension towers, is an icon of the city; opened in 1883, it was the first steel-wire suspension bridge and was the longest suspension bridge in the world until 1903.[[591]](#cite_note-597)[[592]](#cite_note-598) The [Queensboro Bridge](/wiki/Queensboro_Bridge "Queensboro Bridge") "was the longest [cantilever span](/wiki/Cantilever_bridge "Cantilever bridge") in North America" from 1909 to 1917.[[593]](#cite_note-599) The [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), opened in 1909, "is considered to be the forerunner of modern suspension bridges", and its design "served as the model for the major long-span suspension bridges" of the early 20th century.[[594]](#cite_note-600) The [Throgs Neck Bridge](/wiki/Throgs_Neck_Bridge "Throgs Neck Bridge") and [Whitestone Bridge](/wiki/Whitestone_Bridge "Whitestone Bridge") connect Queens and the Bronx, while the [Triborough Bridge](/wiki/Triborough_Bridge "Triborough Bridge") connects Manhattan, Queens, and the Bronx.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Lincoln_Manh_portal_9-38_jeh.JPG/250px-Lincoln_Manh_portal_9-38_jeh.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lincoln_Manh_portal_9-38_jeh.JPG)

[Lincoln Tunnel](/wiki/Lincoln_Tunnel "Lincoln Tunnel")

The [Lincoln Tunnel](/wiki/Lincoln_Tunnel "Lincoln Tunnel"), which carries 120,000 vehicles a day under the Hudson River between New Jersey and Midtown Manhattan, is the busiest vehicular tunnel in the world.[[595]](#cite_note-601) The tunnel was built instead of a bridge to allow unfettered passage of large passenger and [cargo ships](/wiki/Cargo_ship "Cargo ship") that sailed through New York Harbor and up the Hudson River to Manhattan's piers. The [Holland Tunnel](/wiki/Holland_Tunnel "Holland Tunnel"), connecting Lower Manhattan to [Jersey City, New Jersey](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey"), was the first mechanically ventilated vehicular tunnel when it opened in 1927.[[596]](#cite_note-602)[[597]](#cite_note-603) The [Queens–Midtown Tunnel](/wiki/Queens%E2%80%93Midtown_Tunnel "Queens–Midtown Tunnel"), built to relieve congestion on the bridges connecting Manhattan with Queens and Brooklyn, was the largest non-federal project in its time when it was completed in 1940.[[598]](#cite_note-604) The [Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Battery_Tunnel "Brooklyn–Battery Tunnel") (officially the Hugh L. Carey Tunnel) is the longest continuous underwater vehicular tunnel in North America and runs underneath [Battery Park](/wiki/Battery_Park "Battery Park"), connecting the [Financial District, Manhattan](/wiki/Financial_District,_Manhattan "Financial District, Manhattan"), to [Red Hook, Brooklyn](/wiki/Red_Hook,_Brooklyn "Red Hook, Brooklyn").[[599]](#cite_note-605)

## Government and politics

Main articles: [Government of New York City](/wiki/Government_of_New_York_City "Government of New York City"), [Politics of New York City](/wiki/Politics_of_New_York_City "Politics of New York City"), and [Elections in New York City](/wiki/Elections_in_New_York_City "Elections in New York City")

### Government

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/New_York_City_Hall_exterior%2C_October_2016.jpg/250px-New_York_City_Hall_exterior%2C_October_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_City_Hall_exterior,_October_2016.jpg)

[New York City Hall](/wiki/New_York_City_Hall "New York City Hall")

New York City is a [metropolitan municipality](/wiki/Metropolitan_municipality "Metropolitan municipality") with a [strong mayor–council form of government](/wiki/Strong_Mayor "Strong Mayor").[[600]](#cite_note-606) The city government is responsible for public education, correctional institutions, public safety, recreational facilities, sanitation, water supply, and welfare services.

The [City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council") is a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") body of 51 council members whose districts are defined by geographic population boundaries.[[601]](#cite_note-607) Each term for the [mayor](/wiki/Mayor_of_New_York_City "Mayor of New York City") and council members lasts four years and has a two [consecutive-term limit](/wiki/Term_limit "Term limit"),[[602]](#cite_note-608) (reset after a four-year break). The *[New York City Administrative Code](/wiki/New_York_City_Administrative_Code "New York City Administrative Code")*, the *[New York City Rules](/wiki/New_York_City_Rules "New York City Rules")*, and *[The City Record](/wiki/The_City_Record "The City Record")* are the code of local laws, compilation of regulations, and official journal, respectively.[[603]](#cite_note-609)[[604]](#cite_note-610)

Each borough is coextensive with a [judicial district](/wiki/Judicial_district "Judicial district") of the state [Unified Court System](/wiki/New_York_State_Unified_Court_System "New York State Unified Court System"), of which the [Criminal Court](/wiki/New_York_City_Criminal_Court "New York City Criminal Court") and the [Civil Court](/wiki/New_York_City_Civil_Court "New York City Civil Court") are the local courts, while the [New York Supreme Court](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court "New York Supreme Court") conducts major trials and appeals. Manhattan hosts the First Department of the [Supreme Court, Appellate Division](/wiki/New_York_Supreme_Court,_Appellate_Division "New York Supreme Court, Appellate Division"), while Brooklyn hosts the Second Department. There are several extrajudicial [administrative courts](/wiki/Administrative_court "Administrative court"), which are executive agencies and not part of the state Unified Court System.

New York City is divided between, and is host to the main branches of, two different [U.S. district courts](/wiki/U.S._district_courts "U.S. district courts"): the [District Court for the Southern District of New York](/wiki/District_Court_for_the_Southern_District_of_New_York "District Court for the Southern District of New York"), whose main courthouse is on [Foley Square](/wiki/Foley_Square "Foley Square") in Manhattan and whose jurisdiction includes Manhattan and the Bronx;[[605]](#cite_note-611) and the [District Court for the Eastern District of New York](/wiki/District_Court_for_the_Eastern_District_of_New_York "District Court for the Eastern District of New York"), whose main courthouse is in Brooklyn and whose jurisdiction includes Brooklyn, Queens, and Staten Island.[[606]](#cite_note-612) The [U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit](/wiki/U.S._Court_of_Appeals_for_the_Second_Circuit "U.S. Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit") and [U.S. Court of International Trade](/wiki/U.S._Court_of_International_Trade "U.S. Court of International Trade") are also based on Foley Square.[[607]](#cite_note-613)[[608]](#cite_note-614)

### Politics

[![A picture of Zohran Mamdani. He is smiling and looking directly into the camera. He is wearing a black suit and a blue, white spotted tie.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Zohran_Mamdani_05.25.25_%28b%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/330px-Zohran_Mamdani_05.25.25_%28b%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zohran_Mamdani_05.25.25_(b)_(cropped).jpg)

[Zohran Mamdani](/wiki/Zohran_Mamdani "Zohran Mamdani"), the current and 112th [Mayor of New York City](/wiki/Mayor_of_New_York_City "Mayor of New York City")

The city's mayor is [Zohran Mamdani](/wiki/Zohran_Mamdani "Zohran Mamdani"), a [Democrat](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") who was [elected in 2025](/wiki/2025_New_York_City_mayoral_election "2025 New York City mayoral election"). Mamdani took office shortly after midnight in a private ceremony on January 1, 2026.[[609]](#cite_note-615) The Democratic Party holds the majority of public offices. As of November 2023, 67% of active registered voters in the city are Democrats and 10.2% are [Republicans](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)").[[610]](#cite_note-616) New York City has not been carried by a Republican presidential candidate since [1924](/wiki/1924_United_States_presidential_election_in_New_York "1924 United States presidential election in New York"), and no Republican candidate for statewide office has won all five boroughs since the city was incorporated in 1898. In redistricting following the 2020 census, 14 of [New York's 26 congressional districts](/wiki/New_York%27s_congressional_districts "New York's congressional districts") include portions of New York City.[[611]](#cite_note-617)

New York City is a significant source of [political fundraising](/wiki/Political_fundraising "Political fundraising").[[612]](#cite_note-618) The city has a strong imbalance of payments with the national and state governments. It receives 83 cents in services for every $1 it sends to the federal government in [taxes](/wiki/Taxation_in_the_United_States "Taxation in the United States") (or annually sends $11.4 billion more than it receives back). City residents and businesses also sent an additional $4.1 billion in the 2009–2010 [fiscal year](/wiki/Fiscal_year "Fiscal year") to the state than the city received in return.[[613]](#cite_note-619)

"'Leaving their homes,' [immigrants] say, 'with the brightest prospects,' alluring representations presented to them of the blessed state of American life, a few scanty coins in their pockets, though feeling in the enjoyment of rugged health, and surrounded by their young and innocent offspring, little did they imagine the trials to which they would be exposed; but at length they discover to their sorrow, and very natural discontent, that the foul steerage of some ocean-tossed ship is to form the filthy receptacle of persons, crowded too with hordes of human beings, with scarcely space enough to contain the half of them—certainly not more than the *quarter* of them *comfortably*; and thus huddled together *en masse*, they become the "*emigrant passengers*" destined to this country."
